proposals.title: Scaling Masked Autoencoders for Web-Scale Visual Pre-Training with Efficient Large-Batch Optimization
proposals.motivation: The success of foundation models in NLP, trained on web-scale data, has not been fully realized in computer vision. While models like Segment Anything and MAE have shown promise, scaling them to truly internet-sized datasets (e.g., billions of images) remains a major engineering and algorithmic challenge due to prohibitive training costs and instability. Efficient large-batch training is critical to making this scaling feasible within practical compute budgets, but its application to modern vision architectures like Vision Transformers (ViTs) for dense prediction tasks is underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting and rigorously benchmarking large-batch optimization techniques (e.g., LARS, Layer-wise Adaptive Rate Scaling, and gradient clipping) for masked autoencoder (MAE) pre-training on web-scale data, we can achieve superior scaling laws for vision models, leading to foundational backbones that significantly improve performance on downstream segmentation and detection tasks with stable, faster training.
proposals.method: We will build upon the MAE framework and ViT architecture, incorporating insights from 'Early Convolutions Help Transformers See Better' for robust early-stage features. The core technical approach is to systematically implement and evaluate large-batch optimization strategies, scaling batch sizes to tens of thousands on a curated, massive web dataset (e.g., a multi-billion image subset). We will design a staged training protocol, beginning with a convolutional stem, and carefully tune learning rate schedules, optimizer choice (AdamW vs. SGD with LARS), and normalization to maintain stability. The resulting models will be benchmarked on detection and instance segmentation transfer tasks, following the methodology of 'Benchmarking Detection Transfer Learning with Vision Transformers'.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a set of best practices and open-source code for stable large-batch MAE pre-training, 2) a new state-of-the-art pre-trained vision backbone scaled to an unprecedented dataset size, and 3) a comprehensive analysis of scaling laws for vision transformers in the context of dense prediction tasks, quantifying the benefits of data scale versus algorithmic adjustments.
proposals.novelty: While large-batch training is known, its systematic application and optimization for self-supervised MAE-based pre-training on web-scale visual data is novel. The integration of architectural improvements (convolutional stems) with scaled optimization to build a foundational segmentation/ detection model directly addresses a key gap in the transition from NLP-style scaling to vision.

proposals.title: Class-Agnostic Mask Decoupling: Improving Segmentation Generalization by Separating Mask and Class Pathways
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art instance segmentation models, including those from the Mask R-CNN lineage and recent query-based detectors, tightly couple mask prediction with object class prediction. This dependency can limit generalization, especially on novel categories or datasets with long-tailed distributions, as the mask quality is contingent on a potentially erroneous or ambiguous class label. A decoupled approach could lead to more robust and foundational segmentation models, aligning with the 'Segment Anything' goal of general-purpose segmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: Decoupling the mask prediction pathway from the explicit object classification task will produce segmentation models that generalize better to unseen object categories and complex real-world scenes by learning more generic, geometry-focused features.
proposals.method: We propose a modified Mask R-CNN or Mask2Former-style architecture where the mask head receives features exclusively from a dedicated, class-agnostic visual backbone branch, potentially initialized via findings from 'Early Convolutions Help Transformers See Better'. The class prediction branch will operate in parallel, and a lightweight, late-stage fusion module will combine mask and class logics only for final panoptic or instance output if required. The training objective will include a dedicated loss to enforce mask prediction quality independent of the class branch's confidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous analysis of the coupling between classification and segmentation in modern architectures. It will deliver a new, more generalizable model design for instance segmentation and a benchmark demonstrating its improved performance on cross-dataset transfer and few-shot segmentation tasks, extending the 'Benchmarking Detection Transfer Learning' paradigm to segmentation.
proposals.novelty: While class-agnostic detection exists, systematically decoupling and analyzing the mask pathway within high-performance, query-based transformer architectures (like Mask2Former) or refined CNN backbones is unexplored. The novelty lies in the architectural intervention and the hypothesis that such decoupling is beneficial even for within-dataset performance, not just zero-shot transfer.

proposals.title: Mask R-CNN Simplified: A Study of Training Efficiency via Architectural and Optimization Co-design
proposals.motivation: Mask R-CNN remains a foundational model for instance segmentation, but its training is computationally intensive and involves a complex, multi-stage pipeline with carefully tuned hyperparameters. As the field moves towards larger models and datasets, improving the training efficiency and simplifying the recipe for such established architectures is crucial for democratizing research and reducing environmental impact. This work aligns with the researcher's history of improving foundational models like Fast/Faster R-CNN and exploring efficient training dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing specific architectural modifications inspired by recent insights into network initialization and training stability with a streamlined, end-to-end optimization schedule, we can significantly reduce the training cost of Mask R-CNN without sacrificing final performance on COCO.
proposals.method: We will first replace the standard ResNet-FPN backbone with a ViT hybrid initialized using the "Early Convolutions" principle to improve early training stability. We will then systematically simplify the training pipeline: removing cascade refinements, testing a unified learning rate schedule for the backbone and heads, and exploring the efficacy of a single-stage joint optimization for the RPN, box, and mask heads versus the traditional multi-stage scheme. Efficiency will be measured in total GPU hours and iterations to convergence, benchmarked against the standard Detectron2 baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, more efficient training recipe for a cornerstone instance segmentation model, offering clear guidelines for resource-constrained practitioners. It will contribute a rigorous analysis of which components of the complex Mask R-CNN training pipeline are truly necessary, potentially leading to a simpler, more robust standard baseline for future research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on novel architectures or scaling laws, this proposal uniquely targets the *training process* of a mature, specific model (Mask R-CNN), applying recent insights from transformer-convolution hybrid training and optimization simplicity in a focused co-design study.

proposals.title: SAM-Track: Extending Segment Anything to Consistent Video Instance Segmentation
proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) established a powerful foundation model for promptable image segmentation, but its application is inherently frame-by-frame. Video instance segmentation (VIS) requires consistent, temporally stable segmentation and tracking of object identities across frames, a critical capability for video understanding, autonomous systems, and content creation. Extending SAM's capabilities to video addresses a significant gap between static image understanding and dynamic scene analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: That the strong, promptable segmentation priors of SAM can be effectively leveraged and adapted with a lightweight, dedicated temporal reasoning module to achieve robust, consistent video instance segmentation without extensive task-specific retraining.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stream architecture. A frozen SAM image encoder processes individual frames. A parallel, lightweight temporal encoder (e.g., a small ConvLSTM or Transformer) processes frame-level features or SAM's intermediate mask decoder queries. A novel cross-attention fusion module will align and propagate instance-specific embeddings from the temporal stream into SAM's prompt space, conditioning the mask decoder to produce consistent outputs. Training will use a contrastive loss on instance embeddings across frames alongside the standard mask loss, primarily on existing VIS datasets like YouTube-VIS.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new state-of-the-art model for promptable and zero-shot video instance segmentation by grounding it in a foundational vision model. It will provide a principled framework for adding temporal consistency to powerful static image models with minimal parameter overhead. The resulting model and analysis will advance understanding of how to effectively bridge foundational image models and video tasks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior VIS methods built from scratch or fine-tuned detection models, this approach uniquely investigates how to minimally adapt a massive, frozen foundation model (SAM) for temporal consistency, focusing on architectural design for efficient temporal prompt propagation rather than full-model video training.

proposals.title: Adversarial Mask Heads: Improving Segmentation Boundary Quality with Discriminative Feedback
proposals.motivation: Foundation models like Segment Anything (SAM) have democratized segmentation, but their mask outputs can exhibit unrealistic, overly smooth, or 'wiggly' boundaries, especially for complex natural shapes. This limits their utility in applications requiring high geometric fidelity, such as medical imaging, detailed scene understanding, and content creation. Improving boundary realism is a critical step towards segmentation models that produce professional, artifact-free masks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating an adversarial training objective, where a discriminator network is trained to distinguish between real object boundaries (from high-quality datasets) and predicted mask boundaries, will force the segmentation model's mask head to produce sharper, more natural, and geometrically plausible segmentation masks.
proposals.method: We will augment the training pipeline of a SAM-like architecture with a lightweight, patch-based discriminator (e.g., based on Pix2PixHD) that operates on the contour region of predicted masks. The mask head will be trained with a combined loss: the standard segmentation loss (e.g., focal loss, dice loss) plus an adversarial loss from the discriminator. We will explore conditioning the discriminator on image features from the vision encoder to provide context-aware feedback. Training will leverage high-quality segmentation datasets like COCO, LVIS, and potentially specialized boundary datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a method to significantly improve the perceptual quality and geometric accuracy of segmentation mask boundaries from foundation models. We will provide a new benchmark for evaluating mask boundary realism and demonstrate quantitative and qualitative gains on diverse segmentation tasks. The proposed adversarial mask head could become a plug-in module for existing segmentation architectures.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is established for image synthesis, its application is novel for refining the output of a billion-scale, promptable segmentation foundation model like SAM to improve low-level boundary quality, moving beyond standard per-pixel losses.

proposals.title: EdgeMask: A Design-Space Exploration for Real-Time Instance Segmentation on Mobile Devices
proposals.motivation: The demand for on-device instance segmentation is growing for applications like augmented reality and robotics, but the dominant Mask R-CNN framework is too computationally heavy for mobile or edge hardware. Current lightweight segmentation models often sacrifice significant accuracy for speed, creating a performance gap. This work addresses the need for a principled, efficient architecture that maintains robust mask quality under strict latency and memory constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic redesign of the Mask R-CNN components—focusing on backbone, neck, and head efficiency—using pre-2026 architectural principles can yield a model capable of real-time inference on mobile CPUs while retaining competitive accuracy on benchmarks like COCO.
proposals.method: We will conduct a structured design-space exploration, starting with an extremely efficient backbone like a scaled MobileNetV3 or a ShuffleNetV2. We will replace the standard Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) with a computationally lighter BiFPN or a custom single-scale fusion module. For the mask head, we will explore dynamic convolutions and channel-reduced, shallow MLPs. The training will leverage knowledge distillation from a full Mask R-CNN teacher and modern techniques like neural architecture search for optimal layer choices, all optimized via a combined loss of accuracy and a hardware-aware latency penalty.
proposals.contribution: This project will deliver a new, publicly available model family (EdgeMask) that sets a new state-of-the-art for accuracy-latency trade-offs in mobile instance segmentation. We will contribute a detailed analysis of which architectural changes in the segmentation pipeline yield the highest efficiency gains, providing a blueprint for future efficient segmentation design.
proposals.novelty: While efficient backbones exist, a holistic, ground-up redesign of the entire Mask R-CNN system for mobile CPUs—integrating specialized neck/head designs and hardware-in-the-loop NAS—is novel. Our approach moves beyond simple backbone swaps to re-architect the segmentation-specific components for edge deployment.

proposals.title: HybridSparse: Adaptive Multi-Scale Feature Sampling for Efficient Object Detection
proposals.motivation: Modern one-stage detectors like FCOS and YOLO apply dense, uniform sampling across all image regions, leading to significant computational redundancy, especially in simple backgrounds. This inefficiency hinders deployment on resource-constrained devices. A principled method to allocate computation dynamically based on local scene complexity could drastically reduce FLOPs while preserving accuracy on challenging objects.
proposals.hypothesis: A detection framework that uses a lightweight preliminary network to predict a per-image complexity heatmap, which then guides a main detection network to sample features adaptively, can achieve computational efficiency comparable to sparse detectors while maintaining the high accuracy of dense sampling methods, particularly for small and occluded objects.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage architecture. First, a fast, compact ConvNeXt-based network processes a downsampled image to output a 'complexity heatmap' highlighting regions of high object density, small scale, or edge entropy. Second, this heatmap modulates the sampling strategy of a main detection backbone (e.g., a ViT or ConvNet). We will design a novel 'Adaptive Feature Sampling' module that replaces uniform grid sampling with a hybrid strategy: applying dense, multi-scale anchor points in high-complexity zones and a learned, sparse set of points elsewhere. The entire system is trained end-to-end with a loss that balances detection accuracy (e.g., Focal Loss) and a sampling sparsity regularizer.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel object detection framework that dynamically allocates computational resources. We will provide a new benchmark for efficiency-accuracy trade-offs, demonstrating significant FLOP reduction on datasets like COCO. The core 'complexity heatmap' and adaptive sampling module offer a generalizable design principle for efficient vision models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior sparse detectors (e.g., Sparse R-CNN) or dynamic convolutions, our method explicitly learns to predict where dense sampling is necessary from a fast, global analysis of the image, creating a non-uniform, input-dependent sensing strategy inspired by seismic imaging optimization.

proposals.title: Meta-Weighting: Learning to Weight Hard Examples for Dense Prediction via Bi-Level Optimization
proposals.motivation: Handling hard examples is critical for dense prediction tasks like instance segmentation, but heuristics like Focal Loss are static and sub-optimal. A fixed weighting function cannot adapt to the specific difficulty profile of different datasets, architectures, or training stages. A learned, adaptive weighting mechanism could unlock more efficient training and better generalization by directly optimizing for validation performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned weighting function, which dynamically assigns per-element loss weights based on multi-faceted difficulty signals, can discover more effective training strategies than fixed heuristics like Focal Loss, leading to improved generalization on dense prediction tasks.
proposals.method: We instrument a base dense predictor (e.g., Mask R-CNN or a ViT-based Mask Transformer) to extract per-pixel difficulty signals: prediction entropy, gradient magnitude, and consistency across augmentations. A lightweight meta-network (a small MLP or ConvNet) processes these signals to output a dense weighting map. This meta-network is trained via a bi-level optimization loop: the inner loop updates the base model with the weighted loss, and the outer loop updates the meta-network's parameters to minimize the base model's loss on a clean validation set.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, task-adaptive alternative to hand-designed loss functions for hard example mining. It will yield a general meta-weighting framework applicable to various dense prediction tasks (segmentation, detection, pose). The analysis of learned weighting strategies will offer new insights into what makes an example 'hard' during training.
proposals.novelty: Unlike Focal Loss, which uses a fixed parametric form, our method meta-learns a flexible, context-aware weighting function directly from validation data. This moves beyond applying a pre-defined rule to discovering an optimal weighting policy for a given task and model.

proposals.title: MetaBalance: Dynamic Co-Adaptation of Loss and Sampling for Robust Imbalanced Learning
proposals.motivation: Class imbalance remains a critical challenge in real-world vision tasks like long-tailed object detection and segmentation, where standard models favor frequent classes. Pre-2026, solutions like Focal Loss and re-sampling are often applied statically or in isolation, leading to suboptimal or conflicting dynamics during training. A unified, adaptive framework is needed to harmonize these core techniques for more effective and robust learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that meta-learning a controller to dynamically align the parameters of a loss function (e.g., Focal Loss's gamma) with instance sampling probabilities, based on real-time gradient and performance signals, will yield superior performance on imbalanced benchmarks compared to static combinations.
proposals.method: We will design a lightweight meta-controller network (e.g., an MLP) that takes as input per-class gradient statistics and performance metrics (e.g., recall) from a primary task network (e.g., a ConvNeXt or ViT backbone). This controller will output two sets of adaptive parameters per training iteration: 1) the focusing parameter γ for a class-conditioned Focal Loss, and 2) a weighting distribution for instance sampling. The controller will be optimized via a meta-loss on a held-out validation set, using techniques like REINFORCE or a differentiable approximation, to maximize overall validation performance. Training will employ a bi-level optimization loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, unified framework for imbalance correction that dynamically coordinates loss and data manipulation. It will deliver a new state-of-the-art method for long-tailed recognition benchmarks in vision and NLP. The analysis of the learned adaptation policies will offer insights into the training dynamics of imbalanced learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the real-time, learned co-adaptation of loss and sampling, moving beyond their static or sequential application. This treats the imbalance correction strategy as a dynamic control problem optimized via meta-learning, which is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Focal Hinge Loss: A Margin-Sensitive Approach for Imbalanced Classification
proposals.motivation: Class imbalance remains a core challenge in visual recognition, particularly for segmentation and detection tasks where foreground objects are sparse. While focal loss successfully addressed this for cross-entropy-based detectors, its principles have not been systematically adapted to margin-based losses like hinge loss, which are foundational for SVMs and appear in modern max-margin classifiers. This leaves a gap in robust, margin-sensitive learning for imbalanced data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reshaping the hinge loss with a modulating factor that down-weights easy, large-margin examples and up-weights hard, small-margin examples will significantly improve the performance of margin-based classifiers on imbalanced datasets, analogous to the success of focal loss for cross-entropy.
proposals.method: We will formally derive a Focal Hinge Loss by introducing a modulating term (1 - margin)^γ to the standard hinge loss, where 'margin' is the signed distance to the decision boundary. This creates a loss landscape where well-classified examples with large margins contribute negligibly. We will integrate this loss into a simple, scalable linear SVM framework and a deep max-margin network architecture. The core experiments will benchmark against standard hinge loss and cross-entropy variants on long-tailed versions of established segmentation/detection datasets (e.g., LVIS) and synthetic imbalanced benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled adaptation of focal weighting to margin-based losses, expanding the toolkit for imbalanced classification. It will yield a new, interpretable loss function for training robust SVMs and deep max-margin models on real-world, long-tailed data, with direct applications in instance segmentation where class distributions are highly skewed.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit translation of the focal modulation concept from the probability domain (cross-entropy) to the margin domain (hinge loss), a non-trivial reformulation that targets a different family of classifiers. This moves beyond the dominant focus on cross-entropy in the class imbalance literature.

proposals.title: CurriculumDet: Online Self-Paced Learning for Robust Dense Object Detection
proposals.motivation: Dense detection tasks, like instance segmentation, are trained on datasets with immense variation in object scale, occlusion, and appearance. Standard uniform sampling treats all examples equally, wasting compute on trivial cases and potentially destabilizing learning on overly hard ones. An adaptive curriculum, grounded in pre-2026 scaling principles, could optimize the learning trajectory for faster convergence and improved final model robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an online, self-paced curriculum, which dynamically measures per-example difficulty based on the model's instantaneous learning state, will yield more stable optimization and superior final performance in dense detection compared to static or fixed-curriculum training schedules.
proposals.method: We propose a lightweight meta-controller that operates alongside the detection backbone (e.g., a ConvNeXt or ViT-based Mask R-CNN). It continuously estimates per-instance difficulty via metrics like the exponential moving average of loss decay and predictive entropy. This controller uses a bandit-style sampling strategy to construct mini-batches from a prioritized replay buffer, favoring examples within an adaptive 'learning zone' of optimal difficulty. The system will be integrated and evaluated within the Detectron2 framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, plug-in training strategy for dense detection that automates curriculum design. It will provide new insights into the relationship between example difficulty, optimization dynamics, and scaling behavior in modern vision networks. The resulting method is expected to improve both data efficiency and final benchmark performance on tasks like COCO instance segmentation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior curriculum learning that relies on pre-computed heuristics, our method's difficulty metric is defined entirely online and relative to the model's evolving state. This creates a truly self-paced curriculum tailored to the specific network's learning progress, a concept not fully explored for large-scale detection pre-2026.

proposals.title: ProgressiveLoad: A Curriculum Learning Framework for Stable and Efficient Dense Object Detection
proposals.motivation: Training dense object detectors like Mask R-CNN or DETR variants on complex, real-world datasets from scratch is challenging and computationally expensive. Models can struggle with convergence, sensitivity to hyperparameters, and catastrophic forgetting of early-learned simple concepts when faced with a flood of difficult examples. A principled, automated curriculum could address these stability and efficiency issues, which remain open problems in detection model design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structuring detector training with a curriculum that progressively increases task difficulty across multiple axes—object count, scale, occlusion, and background complexity—will lead to more stable convergence, faster training, and higher final accuracy compared to standard random sampling from the full dataset.
proposals.method: We will design a curriculum scheduler that automatically sequences training batches from a dataset like COCO or LVIS. Starting with easy subsets (e.g., images with few, large, unoccluded objects on clean backgrounds), the scheduler will progressively introduce harder examples based on measurable difficulty proxies. We will integrate this into the training loop of a modern detector, such as a ConvNeXt-based Mask R-CNN or a DETR variant, monitoring gradient norms and loss stability to guide the progression pace. The core technical challenge is defining and combining the multi-axis difficulty metrics into a single curriculum policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a general, plug-and-play curriculum learning framework for dense detection tasks, demonstrating measurable improvements in training stability and sample efficiency. It will also provide analysis into which difficulty axes most impact detector learning, offering insights for future dataset design and training protocol development.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning is known, its application to dense detection is underexplored, and a systematic, multi-axis progression based on a sports science analogy is novel. This moves beyond simple data ordering (e.g., by image size) to a controlled simulation of increasingly challenging visual environments.

proposals.title: Curriculum-Driven Architectural Scaling for Dense Detection
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws for vision networks are typically studied in a static, one-shot manner, ignoring the dynamic nature of learning. In dense detection, where data complexity varies enormously, this static approach may be suboptimal for sample efficiency and final performance. A principled method to co-evolve network architecture with a data curriculum could unlock more efficient scaling pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal scaling pathway (in width, depth, and cardinality) for a dense detection network is not static but is dependent on the difficulty of the data being learned; synchronizing architectural growth with a data difficulty curriculum will yield superior sample efficiency and Pareto-optimal performance compared to fixed-architecture training.
proposals.method: We will extend the Mask R-CNN/RetinaNet frameworks, integrating a curriculum scheduler that sequences data from COCO and LVIS by difficulty (e.g., using instance size, occlusion, or classifier confidence). In parallel, a controller will modulate the backbone (e.g., a ConvNeXt or hybrid Conv-Transformer) and detection head dimensions. Starting from a small seed network, we will use a policy gradient or evolutionary search to grow specific dimensions (e.g., increase width for harder classes, add depth for complex scenes) in response to performance plateaus on the current curriculum tier, creating a joint optimization of architecture and learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel training paradigm that unifies curriculum learning with architectural scaling, yielding practical recipes for efficient model development. It will also contribute new insights into dynamic, data-dependent scaling laws for dense visual recognition, moving beyond static scaling rules.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning and neural scaling laws are established, their synergistic, dynamic co-adaptation is unexplored. Proposing to let the architecture itself 'grow' strategically in sync with data complexity is a novel departure from fixed-architecture training and one-shot scaling studies.

proposals.title: Gradient Load Balancer: Dynamic Gradient Harmonization for Object Detection via Distributed Systems Analogy
proposals.motivation: Object detection models suffer from severe optimization imbalances, including foreground-background class imbalance and varying per-sample difficulty. Pre-2026 solutions like Focal Loss or Gradient Harmonizing Mechanism (GHM) use static or pre-computed re-weighting, which may not adapt optimally to the non-stationary gradient distributions encountered during training. This leads to suboptimal convergence and performance, especially in long-tailed or complex multi-task detection settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the gradient flow during backpropagation as a distributed computational load and applying real-time, adaptive load-balancing algorithms will lead to more stable optimization and improved final performance in object detection.
proposals.method: We will design a Gradient Load Balancer (GLB) module that sits in the backward pass. Inspired by algorithms like Weighted Least Connections, it will dynamically compute a weight for each gradient element (e.g., per-class or per-instance) based on real-time statistics of gradient magnitude and frequency over a rolling window. This weight will be used to rescale gradients before the optimizer step. We will integrate GLB into standard detection frameworks (e.g., Mask R-CNN, DETR variants) and benchmark it on COCO, LVIS, and Object365, comparing against static re-weighting baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, adaptive paradigm for gradient handling in deep learning, directly translating principles from distributed systems. It will provide a robust, plug-and-play module for improving detection model training, particularly for datasets with inherent imbalances. We will release code and pre-trained models to facilitate adoption.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual analogy and technical translation of dynamic load-balancing algorithms from distributed computing to the deep learning optimization loop, moving beyond static statistical re-weighting to a responsive, state-aware gradient modulation system.

proposals.title: Gradient Harmonized Detector: Balancing Loss Gradients for Robust Object Detection
proposals.motivation: Object detection models, especially in long-tailed or complex multi-task settings, suffer from imbalanced gradients during training. This imbalance stems from the uneven distribution of foreground/background samples and across object categories, leading to suboptimal convergence and performance. While Gradient Harmonizing Mechanisms (GHM) have shown promise in classification, their principled adaptation to the multi-task loss landscape of modern detectors remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly harmonizing gradients across the classification, regression, and potentially segmentation tasks within a detector's loss function will stabilize training, improve convergence speed, and boost final accuracy, particularly on rare classes and challenging localization cases.
proposals.method: We will adapt the GHM-C and GHM-R loss formulations to the specific components of a standard detection framework (e.g., Faster R-CNN or a Transformer-based DETR variant). This involves analyzing the gradient density for both classification logits and bounding box regression targets during training. We will design a task-aware harmonizing mechanism that dynamically re-weights samples based on gradient magnitudes across all loss heads. The implementation will be integrated into the Detectron2 codebase for rigorous benchmarking on LVIS (long-tailed) and COCO datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, gradient-aware loss design principle for object detection, leading to more robust and efficient training. We expect to deliver a simple, plug-in module that improves state-of-the-art detectors, with quantifiable gains on tail categories and overall mAP. The analysis will offer new insights into the training dynamics of multi-task vision networks.
proposals.novelty: While GHM is known for classification, its direct application to the joint optimization of classification and regression in detection is non-trivial and novel. The core innovation is a holistic, gradient-based re-weighting scheme that operates across different task-specific loss functions within a single model, moving beyond simple class-frequency balancing.

proposals.title: Boosting the Detector: Adaptive Hard Example Mining via Iterative Loss Re-weighting
proposals.motivation: Modern object detectors, while powerful, often exhibit uneven performance across object classes, scales, and contexts. Standard training with uniform sampling or static hard example mining fails to adapt to a model's evolving, specific failure modes throughout optimization. This leads to sub-optimal performance on 'long-tail' or challenging scenarios, which is a critical bottleneck for real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a boosting-inspired, iterative re-weighting mechanism to a single detector's training loop will allow it to adaptively focus on its current weaknesses, leading to more robust and uniformly improved performance without requiring an ensemble of models.
proposals.method: We will integrate a boosting meta-algorithm into the training of a standard detector (e.g., Mask R-CNN or a ViT-based detector). After each training epoch, we will perform a detailed error analysis on a held-out validation set to compute per-example, per-class, and per-attribute (scale, occlusion) loss statistics. These statistics will inform a multiplicative weight for each training example in the subsequent epoch's loss function, directly increasing the influence of currently hard-to-learn data. The framework will be designed to be architecture-agnostic, building on pre-2026 work in gradient boosting and online hard example mining.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, automated training strategy that systematically addresses performance disparities in object detection. It will yield a practical method for improving model robustness, particularly on under-represented or difficult cases, and offer new insights into the optimization dynamics of deep detectors.
proposals.novelty: While hard example mining exists, framing detector training as an internal boosting process—where the model iteratively re-weights its own training distribution based on a self-diagnosis of errors—is a novel adaptation of ensemble theory to single-model optimization.

proposals.title: RetinaNet-XPA: Cross-Path Attention for Lightweight Object Detection
proposals.motivation: Deploying accurate object detectors on mobile and edge devices is constrained by severe computational and memory budgets. While lightweight backbones exist, detection-specific architectures like RetinaNet often rely on standard, heavy FPN and head modules. There is a need for novel, efficient detection architectures that go beyond simple backbone scaling to achieve a superior accuracy-efficiency trade-off for real-world edge applications.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting an extremely lightweight RetinaNet backbone with multi-branch blocks featuring selective, cross-path attention will enable dynamic integration of multi-scale contextual features, significantly improving detection accuracy for a fixed parameter and FLOP budget compared to standard efficient convolutional designs.
proposals.method: We will design a family of lightweight RetinaNet variants. The core building block is a multi-branch 'Fusion Block' where parallel paths (e.g., depthwise convolutions, dilated convolutions, identity) process features at different receptive fields or abstractions. A novel lightweight Cross-Path Attention (XPA) module, inspired by simplified channel attention and gating mechanisms (e.g., SE-like but cross-branch), will be inserted to compute adaptive weights for fusing the branch outputs. These blocks will replace standard conv layers in the backbone and potentially the detection head. We will rigorously benchmark variants on COCO against MobileNetV3-based RetinaNet and other contemporary efficient detectors.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, highly efficient detection architecture family with published models and code. It will provide a systematic analysis of cross-path feature fusion for detection efficiency, establishing a new strong baseline for mobile object detection. The proposed XPA mechanism offers a general, plug-in design principle for efficient multi-branch networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit design of lightweight, attention-based communication between parallel processing paths *within* a block for detection, moving beyond static fusion or post-hoc feature pyramid integration. This targets dynamic, in-network multi-scale context aggregation tailored for constrained devices.

proposals.title: SparseProbe: End-to-End Learning for Efficient Object Detection in Gigapixel Images
proposals.motivation: Analyzing gigapixel images, such as whole-slide pathology scans or satellite imagery, is computationally prohibitive for standard object detectors. Current multi-stage methods often rely on heuristic region selection or separate, non-differentiable pipelines, limiting their accuracy and adaptability. An efficient, end-to-end trainable system for sparse object detection in ultra-high-resolution contexts is a critical unsolved problem in computer vision.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight, learnable 'sparsity probe' network can be trained end-to-end with a high-resolution detector to efficiently and adaptively identify critical regions, making accurate gigapixel-scale detection computationally feasible without sacrificing performance.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage, fully differentiable architecture. First, a lightweight convolutional Sparsity Probe Network (SPN), inspired by efficient design principles from works like 'Early Convolutions Help Transformers See Better', performs a fast, low-resolution pass over the downsampled gigapixel image to predict sparse, salient region proposals. These proposals are used to extract high-resolution Region of Interest (RoI) patches from the original image. A high-capacity detection network (e.g., a Vision Transformer-based detector) processes only these patches. The entire system is trained end-to-end using a combined loss, where gradients from the detector's focal loss backpropagate through the RoI sampling to guide the SPN, akin to an attention mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, end-to-end framework for gigapixel object detection that dramatically reduces computational cost compared to monolithic processing. It will provide a new benchmark for efficiency and accuracy on large-scale pathology and remote sensing datasets. The learnable sparsity probe introduces a new paradigm for adaptive computation in high-resolution visual recognition.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior sliding-window or heuristic proposal methods, our core novelty is the end-to-end differentiability of the sparse region proposal mechanism, allowing the probe network to learn an optimal data-dependent sparsity pattern directly from the detection objective, which is non-trivial to optimize.

proposals.title: GigaDet: Scaling RetinaNet for Efficient Instance Detection in Gigapixel Images
proposals.motivation: The analysis of gigapixel images from satellite, microscopy, and medical imaging is critical for scientific discovery and industrial applications. However, existing object detectors like RetinaNet, designed for standard-resolution photos, fail on gigapixel data due to extreme object sparsity, massive memory requirements, and inefficient processing of mostly empty scenes. A scalable, efficient solution is needed to unlock the potential of ultra-high-resolution visual data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-scale, patch-based detection framework, combined with a heavily re-calibrated Focal Loss and a novel sparse feature aggregation module, can enable RetinaNet to achieve state-of-the-art detection performance on gigapixel imagery with practical computational costs.
proposals.method: We will adapt RetinaNet by implementing a hierarchical inference pipeline: a lightweight 'guide network' first identifies candidate patches containing objects, which are then processed at high resolution by a 'detection network'. We will introduce a 'Sparse Region-of-Interest Pooling' layer to efficiently aggregate context from large, sparse areas. Crucially, we will reformulate Focal Loss parameters (alpha, gamma) to address the extreme foreground-background imbalance (e.g., 1:10^5) inherent to gigapixel scenes, informed by a statistical analysis of object distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source, scalable detection framework specifically optimized for gigapixel images, establishing a new benchmark for the task. It will provide novel insights into loss function design for extreme class imbalance and feature aggregation for sparse detection, advancing the field of large-scale visual recognition.
proposals.novelty: While patch-based processing is common, the co-design of a sparse patch-selection mechanism with a fundamentally re-weighted Focal Loss tailored for gigapixel statistics is novel. This moves beyond simple fine-tuning to a holistic architectural and optimization redesign for a new data regime.

proposals.title: Scaling Vision Models via Adversarial Hard Example Synthesis with Focal Loss
proposals.motivation: Scaling vision models requires ever-larger, more diverse datasets, but data collection and annotation are major bottlenecks. Current data scaling strategies often rely on web-crawled data of inconsistent quality, lacking targeted examples that expose model weaknesses. A method to automatically synthesize maximally informative training data could directly address scaling inefficiencies and improve robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that performing gradient ascent on a trained model's focal loss to synthesize 'hard' adversarial images, and then retraining the model on these examples, will create a self-improving curriculum that systematically strengthens the model and improves scaling behavior more efficiently than scaling data alone.
proposals.method: We will adapt gradient-based generative techniques from domains like molecule design. Starting from noise or natural image seeds, we will perform iterative gradient ascent directly on pixel values to maximize the focal loss of a fixed, pre-trained classification model (e.g., a ConvNeXt or Vision Transformer). This synthesizes novel images at the model's decision boundary. We will integrate these synthesized hard examples into the training pipeline, retraining the model on a mixture of original and synthetic data. The process will be repeated, creating a curriculum of increasing difficulty.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel data synthesis framework for targeted model scaling, moving beyond passive data collection. It will provide new insights into the failure modes of large-scale vision models and demonstrate a path to more sample-efficient scaling. The resulting 'hard example' datasets will serve as a valuable benchmark for robustness.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial attacks modify existing images, this method generates novel, synthetic hard examples from scratch via loss maximization, framing it as a data synthesis problem. The explicit connection to scaling bottlenecks and the use of focal loss for generation, inspired by cross-domain analogies, is unexplored in pre-2026 computer vision.

proposals.title: Cardinality-Guided Adversarial Synthesis: A Closed Loop for Validating Feature Space Organization and Improving Model Robustness
proposals.motivation: Recent work on vision backbones, including ConvNeXt and early-convolution hybrids, highlights the importance of architectural choices like cardinality (the size of transformation sets in grouped convolutions). However, the theoretical understanding of why these designs work is often post-hoc. Concurrently, adversarial example generation exposes model weaknesses but is typically untethered from architectural theory. Bridging this gap is crucial for principled, robust model design.
proposals.hypothesis: The feature space organization induced by a model's cardinality structure defines a predictable manifold of hard examples; synthesizing examples constrained to this manifold will both validate the theoretical model of cardinality and create targeted adversarial data that fortifies the model against systematic failures.
proposals.method: We will develop a synthesis framework where a generator (e.g., a StyleGAN2-based model) is conditioned on the feature gradients of a target model (like a ConvNeXt variant) computed via a cardinality-aware version of focal loss. A theoretical regularizer, derived from group transformation invariance properties linked to cardinality, will constrain the adversarial search space. The synthesized images will be used to probe the model, with results feeding into an updated theoretical bound on robustness, which then refines the regularizer for the next synthesis round.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel methodology for generating theoretically-grounded hard examples that target specific architectural priors. 2) Empirical validation and refinement of the theoretical principles behind cardinality in modern ConvNets and hybrids. 3) A 'fortified' model checkpoint with improved robustness on a new benchmark of cardinality-aware adversarial examples.
proposals.novelty: This work uniquely closes the loop between empirical adversarial synthesis and architectural theory, moving beyond using cardinality as just a design hyperparameter to treating it as a theoretical construct that can be actively probed and validated.

proposals.title: Focal Synthesis: Generating Hard Examples via Adversarial Loss Landscapes for Robust Segmentation
proposals.motivation: Current segmentation models, including foundation models like Segment Anything, are trained on large, static datasets. This process is inefficient, as many examples are easy and contribute little to learning. A core principle from focal loss is that hard examples are crucial for final model performance, but actively finding or creating them remains an open challenge, limiting training efficiency and robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: The gradient of a focal loss-based training objective can be used to define an adversarial synthesis process that generates maximally hard training images, and training on these synthesized hard examples will lead to more robust and data-efficient segmentation models.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage, iterative framework. First, we use a pre-trained generator (e.g., a StyleGAN variant) and a frozen target segmentation model. We then perform adversarial optimization on the generator's latent space, maximizing the focal loss computed on the model's predictions for the generated image and a target pseudo-mask. This creates 'Focal Hard Examples' (FHEs). Second, we interleave these FHEs with standard training data in each batch, continually updating the segmentation model against its own evolving failure modes. The process draws on adversarial machine learning and differentiable augmentation techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, loss-driven data synthesis paradigm for segmentation. It will provide a methodology for creating compact, challenging training sets that improve model robustness, particularly on rare object categories and complex boundaries. We will release a benchmark of generated hard examples and demonstrate improved data efficiency on transfer learning tasks for instance and object segmentation.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training and data augmentation exist, directly inverting the focal loss to *synthesize* hard visual examples, rather than just perturbing existing ones, is a novel direction. This moves beyond using the loss for weighting samples to using it as a creative signal for generative models.

proposals.title: Edge-LTR: Adaptive ResNeXt and Dynamic Focal Loss for Efficient Long-Tail Recognition
proposals.motivation: Real-world vision tasks on edge devices, from wildlife monitoring to industrial inspection, involve hundreds of categories with extreme, long-tailed distributions. Current solutions are bifurcated: efficient architectures (e.g., MobileNetV3) ignore severe class imbalance, while class-balancing losses (e.g., Focal Loss) are designed for large, server-side models. This leaves a critical gap for deploying accurate, compact models on resource-constrained hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: The joint, adaptive optimization of a model's architectural groups and its loss function's focusing parameter—conditioned on runtime constraints and batch statistics—will enable a single efficient network to achieve superior accuracy on both head and tail classes under strict latency/energy budgets.
proposals.method: We will design an adaptive ResNeXt variant where the cardinality (number of grouped convolution paths) and group width are dynamically pruned via a hardware-aware neural architecture search, targeting a specific device's latency/energy profile. Concurrently, we will develop a Dynamic Focal Loss where the focusing parameter γ is modulated per mini-batch by a lightweight meta-network, using inputs of class frequency, per-class average logits, and the current architectural compression ratio. The model and loss will be co-optimized end-to-end on long-tailed datasets like iNaturalist 2021 and Open Images.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel, tunable ResNeXt architecture for edge devices that explicitly trades off group complexity for speed. 2) A Dynamic Focal Loss formulation that responds to both data statistics and model state. 3) Empirical evidence that this co-design surpasses isolated efficient-backbone or loss-reweighting baselines on long-tailed benchmarks under hardware constraints.
proposals.novelty: This is the first work to tightly couple the architectural degree-of-freedom (grouped convolutions) with a dynamically parameterized imbalance loss, where both are governed by a target hardware budget, creating a fully adaptive system for edge long-tail recognition.

proposals.title: GradAlign: Dynamic Gradient Coordination for Robust Multi-Task Dense Prediction
proposals.motivation: Multi-task learning for dense prediction (e.g., detection, segmentation, depth) is crucial for efficient, holistic scene understanding, but is often hindered by conflicting gradients during joint optimization. Pre-2026 methods like uncertainty weighting or GradNorm offer static or magnitude-based balancing, but fail to address directional conflicts that lead to sub-optimal performance and task dominance. A principled, dynamic method for resolving gradient interference is needed to unlock the full potential of multi-task vision models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a real-time, gradient-coordination layer that analyzes and resolves directional conflicts between task-specific gradients will enable more balanced and synergistic learning, leading to superior performance across all tasks compared to existing loss-balancing techniques.
proposals.method: We propose a Gradient Coordination Layer (GCL) inserted before the shared backbone's parameter updates. During each training step, the GCL computes task-specific gradients for the shared parameters. It then applies a conflict resolution mechanism: first, it identifies conflicting gradient directions via pairwise cosine similarity analysis; second, it dynamically adjusts gradient contributions using a combination of projection (to remove conflicting components) and adaptive re-weighting (based on gradient magnitude and task progress). This system will be integrated into a ConvNeXt or ViT-based multi-task architecture, trained on a combined dataset of COCO, ADE20K, and NYUv2.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, training-time mechanism for gradient conflict resolution in dense prediction. It will deliver a new state-of-the-art multi-task model benchmarked against strong baselines like Invariant Risk Minimization and PCGrad. The code and analysis of gradient interactions will serve as a valuable tool for the community.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that primarily scales loss magnitudes, our method directly manipulates gradient vectors in real-time based on directional conflict analysis. This moves beyond static balancing to a dynamic, geometry-aware coordination process tailored for heterogeneous dense tasks.

proposals.title: FPN++: A Unified Multi-Scale Feature Pyramid Architecture for Dense Prediction Tasks
proposals.motivation: The Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) has become a cornerstone for multi-scale feature representation in object detection, but its application to other dense prediction tasks like semantic segmentation and human pose estimation remains underexplored. As the field moves towards unified architectures for multi-task learning, a systematic investigation of FPN's design principles beyond detection is crucial. This work aims to establish a generalized, task-agnostic FPN framework that can improve accuracy and efficiency across a spectrum of pixel-wise prediction problems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core FPN design of top-down pathway and lateral connections can be effectively generalized and optimized to serve as a superior multi-scale backbone for a wide range of dense prediction tasks, outperforming task-specific architectures. Furthermore, we posit that a unified FPN design can facilitate effective knowledge transfer across different tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the standard FPN architecture for semantic segmentation (on Cityscapes and ADE20K) and human pose estimation (on COCO Keypoints), modifying the prediction heads accordingly. We will conduct a rigorous ablation study on backbone networks (ResNet, ConvNeXt, Vision Transformers with hybrid early convolutions), fusion operations (sum vs. concatenation), and the granularity of pyramid levels. The study will benchmark against established baselines like U-Net and HRNet, measuring accuracy, parameter efficiency, and inference speed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive analysis and a set of best practices for using FPN across major dense prediction tasks. It will deliver strong, efficient baseline models for segmentation and keypoint estimation, and offer insights into how multi-scale feature design principles transfer between different problem domains. The resulting unified architecture could simplify the design of multi-task learning systems.
proposals.novelty: While FPN is ubiquitous in detection, its systematic evaluation and tailored adaptation for segmentation and keypoint estimation is novel. The investigation into its synergy with modern backbones like early-convolution ViTs for these tasks, and the pursuit of a single, generalizable multi-scale feature design, moves beyond the current paradigm of task-specific architectures.

proposals.title: PyramidMT: Multi-Task Feature Learning for Scale-Aware Visual Recognition
proposals.motivation: Feature pyramid networks (FPNs) are a cornerstone of modern multi-scale recognition systems, but they treat each pyramid level as a separate, isolated pathway for a single task. This design misses an opportunity for cross-scale and cross-task synergy, which is crucial for complex, multi-scale tasks like instance segmentation and object detection. A principled multi-task learning framework across pyramid levels could lead to more efficient and powerful representations, especially as models scale in size and capability.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly formulating feature learning at each pyramid level as a multi-task problem—where tasks include level-specific prediction and auxiliary self-supervised objectives—we can learn more generalized and robust features that improve performance across diverse visual recognition tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a pyramid network where each level's feature map is processed by a shared transformer or convolutional encoder, followed by specialized adapters for primary tasks (e.g., detection, segmentation). Crucially, we will introduce auxiliary, scale-specific self-supervised tasks (e.g., jigsaw puzzle reconstruction, relative position prediction) applied to features at each level. A dynamic gradient modulation mechanism, inspired by uncertainty weighting, will balance the losses from different tasks and levels. The architecture will be evaluated on COCO and LVIS for instance segmentation and object detection, building directly on the Mask R-CNN and ViTDet frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new architectural paradigm for multi-scale visual recognition that systematically leverages multi-task learning. We will deliver a scalable, open-source model that demonstrates improved parameter efficiency and performance on standard benchmarks. The analysis will yield insights into how features specialize and generalize across scales in a multi-task setting.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning and feature pyramids are well-studied independently, their explicit integration—treating each pyramid level as a multi-task learning hub with auxiliary self-supervision—is unexplored. This moves beyond simple weight sharing to a structured, learnable framework for cross-scale feature specialization and generalization.

proposals.title: FPN for Scale-Stable Detection: Leveraging Multi-Scale Features for Single-Scale Object Detection
proposals.motivation: Feature Pyramid Networks (FPNs) are a cornerstone of modern object detection, primarily used to handle multi-scale objects. However, many real-world datasets, such as those in medical imaging, aerial surveillance, or industrial inspection, exhibit limited scale variation. The standard practice for such datasets is to use a simpler, single-scale backbone, potentially discarding the rich, multi-level feature representations that an FPN can provide, even when scale invariance is not the primary challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hierarchical, semantically diverse features from an FPN, when aggregated effectively for a single-scale detection task, can provide a richer representation than a single-scale backbone, leading to improved accuracy and robustness on datasets with limited scale variation.
proposals.method: We will adapt a standard FPN architecture (e.g., on top of a ResNet backbone) for single-scale detection. Instead of assigning objects to specific FPN levels based on size, we will explore feature fusion strategies to create a single, enhanced feature map for the detector head. Specifically, we will investigate and benchmark: 1) weighted fusion (e.g., using attention or learned weights) of all FPN levels, 2) adaptive selection of the most relevant level per instance via a lightweight routing network, and 3) dense connections between FPN levels prior to fusion. The fused feature map will be used with a standard single-stage detector head (e.g., FCOS or ATSS). Experiments will be conducted on curated single-scale subsets of COCO and domain-specific datasets like VisDrone.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic analysis of FPN's utility beyond multi-scale detection, establishing best practices for leveraging pyramidal features in scale-stable contexts. It will deliver a simple, plug-and-play module to boost performance on specialized detection tasks where object scale is relatively consistent, broadening the application of well-understood architectures.
proposals.novelty: While FPNs are used ubiquitously for scale variation, their explicit redesign and evaluation for the *dual problem* of single-scale detection is unexplored. The novelty lies in treating the FPN not as a scale-handling module but as a generic feature enrichment engine for a narrower but common problem setting.

proposals.title: SAM-Annotate: An Interactive, Model-in-the-Loop Framework for Efficient Instance Segmentation Annotation
proposals.motivation: The success of large-scale models like Segment Anything (SAM) hinges on massive, high-quality annotated datasets. However, creating such datasets for instance segmentation and detection remains prohibitively slow and expensive, acting as a primary bottleneck for scaling vision models to new domains and tasks. Current crowd-sourcing interfaces are often generic and do not leverage the predictive power of modern foundation models to actively assist the annotator.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an interactive annotation interface, which tightly integrates a segmentation foundation model's predictions into a real-time, corrective feedback loop, can significantly reduce annotation time and mental load while improving label consistency for instance segmentation tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a web-based tool centered on a 'predict-correct' paradigm. The core technical approach leverages the Segment Anything Model (SAM) as a backend server. The interface will present the annotator with an image and SAM's initial mask proposals. The annotator's interactions (clicks, scribbles, box adjustments) will be sent as real-time prompts to SAM via its promptable inference API, with the updated mask overlaid instantly. We will implement efficient interaction primitives inspired by RITM and f-BRS for iterative refinement, and design a task pipeline that intelligently sequences object annotation to minimize redundant prompts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) an open-source, production-ready annotation interface specifically optimized for instance segmentation; 2) a quantitative benchmark measuring annotation speed and accuracy gains compared to traditional tools like LabelMe and CVAT; 3) empirical insights into human-AI collaboration patterns for dense prediction tasks, informing future model and interface design.
proposals.novelty: While SAM is a powerful promptable segmenter, its use has been largely model-centric. Our novelty lies in systematically designing the human-in-the-loop system around it, creating a seamless, interactive workflow that treats the model as an active annotation co-pilot rather than a static pre-processor.

proposals.title: Scene-Centric Pre-training: A Data and Architecture Framework for Holistic Visual Understanding
proposals.motivation: Modern visual recognition systems, including foundational models like Segment Anything, are predominantly pre-trained on object-centric datasets (e.g., ImageNet) or web-scale image-text pairs that emphasize object nouns. This biases networks towards local object features, often at the expense of holistic scene properties like layout, texture, and global context, which are crucial for high-level scene classification and understanding. A scene-centric pre-training paradigm is needed to correct this bias and improve performance on tasks where the whole is greater than the sum of its parts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-training vision networks on a large-scale, scene-classified dataset with a tailored architecture that explicitly models global interactions will yield foundational features superior for holistic scene understanding tasks, without degrading fine-grained object recognition capabilities.
proposals.method: We will first curate 'SceneNet-10M', a large-scale dataset by filtering and classifying web images using a strong, pre-2026 scene classifier (e.g., Places-365 fine-tuned). Architecturally, we will adapt the ConvNeXt or a hybrid Conv-Transformer design, informed by Dollár's work on early convolutions, but integrate a 'Scene Context Module'—a lightweight, parallel transformer pathway operating on low-resolution feature maps to capture long-range dependencies. Pre-training will use a joint objective combining scene classification and a novel 'scene consistency' contrastive loss that enforces invariance to object-level augmentations while being sensitive to scene-structure changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a new large-scale scene-centric pre-training dataset and benchmark, 2) a demonstrably more effective backbone for scene classification, retrieval, and weakly-supervised segmentation tasks, and 3) analysis showing how object- vs. scene-centric features complement each other in a unified model.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically inverting the dominant object-centric pre-training paradigm by constructing both the data and the model architecture from first principles to prioritize scene holism, moving beyond simply using scene classification as a fine-tuning task on object-trained models.

proposals.title: Scene Graph from Anything: A Unified Model for Panoptic, Geometric, and Relational Scene Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current vision models excel at isolated tasks like object detection or segmentation but lack a unified, compositional understanding of scenes. For applications in robotics and augmented reality, a model must jointly infer object identities, spatial extents, 3D layout, and their semantic relationships. Pre-2026, models like Segment Anything provide powerful segmentation primitives, but do not explicitly model relationships or 3D context, creating a critical gap.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, end-to-end trainable model, built upon a unified scene representation, can jointly and more accurately perform panoptic segmentation, estimate coarse geometry, and predict object relationships by leveraging the synergistic constraints between these tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a transformer-based architecture with a shared encoder (e.g., a ViT-Hybrid from 'Early Convolutions...') and multiple task-specific decoders. The core is a 'scene graph decoder' that operates on learnable scene entity queries. Each query is tasked with predicting an object's class, mask (extending SAM's promptable mask decoder), a coarse 3D bounding box (from monocular cues), and directed edges to other queries representing predicate relationships. A novel 'relational attention' module will condition relationship prediction on both semantic features and predicted spatial/geometric layouts.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-of-its-kind model for holistic scene parsing, outputting a rich scene graph with geometric attributes. We will introduce a new benchmark combining existing datasets (COCO, Visual Genome, depth datasets) for evaluation. The unified architecture provides a scalable path toward more coherent and useful scene representations for downstream applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats segmentation, depth estimation, and scene graph generation separately, this integrates them into one model where geometric and relational reasoning are co-dependent. The use of a query-based transformer to unify these diverse outputs into a single structured representation is a significant departure from multi-stage pipelines.

proposals.title: HybridConv: A Hybrid Convolution-Transformer Architecture for Instance Segmentation with Deformable Part Priors
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of foundational models like Segment Anything (SAM), achieving high-performance instance segmentation on specific detection benchmarks often requires surpassing classical but robust baselines like Deformable Parts Models (DPMs). DPMs encode valuable structural priors about object parts and deformation that are not fully leveraged by modern, purely data-driven architectures. Integrating these inductive biases into scalable, contemporary networks could yield more data-efficient and accurate segmentation models, particularly in data-limited scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly incorporating deformable part-based structural priors into a hybrid convolution-transformer backbone, initialized using insights from early convolution benefits, will significantly improve instance segmentation accuracy over standard DPM and pure transformer baselines.
proposals.method: We propose a HybridConv architecture that uses a convolutional stem (inspired by 'Early Convolutions Help Transformers See Better') for robust low-level feature extraction, followed by a transformer encoder-decoder for global context. Crucially, we introduce a 'Deformable Part Attention' module that learns a sparse set of deformable part keypoints, regularizing the transformer's self-attention to focus on semantically meaningful object substructures. This module will be integrated into the decoder, using a multi-task loss combining standard mask prediction with a part localization auxiliary task, trained end-to-end on COCO and LVIS.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, high-performance architecture for instance segmentation that formally bridges classical deformable part models and modern neural networks. It will provide new insights into how structural priors can be injected into large-scale models to improve data efficiency and final accuracy, advancing the design principles for segmentation networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, learnable integration of deformable part model concepts as an attention-based regularization mechanism within a transformer framework, moving beyond simple architectural hybrid designs to a deeper synthesis of classical and modern computer vision principles.

proposals.title: Segment Anything, Cheaply: Scaling Segmentation Datasets via Weak Supervision and Task-Specific Priors
proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything (SA) project demonstrated the power of a massive, diverse segmentation dataset (SA-1B) but relied on an expensive, iterative 'data engine' requiring model-in-the-loop human annotation. This cost and complexity hinder the creation of similarly scaled datasets for new domains or more specialized tasks. A method to generate high-quality segmentation masks at scale without this interactive loop is a critical open problem for democratizing foundation model development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging a diverse portfolio of existing, off-the-shelf vision models (e.g., saliency detectors, unsupervised object discovery, edge detectors, pretrained backbones) as weak supervisors, and integrating them with simple, task-informed spatial priors, we can generate segmentation masks of sufficient quality and scale to train a robust, general-purpose segmentation model that rivals one trained on SA-1B.
proposals.method: We will design a weak supervision framework that aggregates noisy signals from a fixed set of pre-2026 foundation models and low-level vision algorithms. Inspired by the design principles in 'Early Convolutions Help Transformers See Better', we will use a ConvNet-based fusion module to spatially integrate these multi-scale, heterogeneous signals. A key innovation is the injection of lightweight, parameterized spatial priors (e.g., encouraging convexity or connectivity) learned from small curated datasets, to guide the aggregation and refine the final mask proposals. This system will be applied to large, unlabeled image corpora to auto-generate a new dataset, 'Weakly-Supervised SA' (WS-SA).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) A novel, cost-effective pipeline for generating large-scale segmentation data, breaking the dependency on interactive annotation. 2) The WS-SA dataset and a benchmark analysis comparing models trained on WS-SA versus the original SA-1B, following the rigorous evaluation ethos of 'Benchmarking Detection Transfer Learning'. 3) New insights into the sufficiency of weak signals for training high-performance segmentation networks.
proposals.novelty: While weak supervision is established, its application to generate *general-purpose* segmentation data at the scale and diversity of SA-1B is unexplored. The novelty lies in the systematic integration of diverse, fixed weak supervisors with learnable task-specific spatial regularizers, moving beyond a single model-in-the-loop to a scalable, parallelizable data factory.

proposals.title: Segmentation with Scalable, Efficient Convolutional Networks (SE-ConvSAM)
proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) established a powerful promptable segmentation paradigm but relies on a Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone, which is computationally intensive for high-resolution inputs and lacks the inherent spatial inductive bias of convolutions. As segmentation is a dense prediction task requiring efficient, multi-scale feature extraction, there is a clear need to explore high-performance, scalable alternatives that can match or exceed SAM's quality while being more efficient to train and deploy.
proposals.hypothesis: A modern, large-scale convolutional network backbone, designed with insights from recent architectural advances, can achieve segmentation quality and promptability comparable to SAM's ViT-H, while offering superior training and inference efficiency on dense image data.
proposals.method: We will design a new convolutional backbone, SE-ConvSAM, building upon the principles of the ConvNeXt architecture but tailored for segmentation. The core design will integrate: 1) A hierarchical, multi-stage feature pyramid structure for dense prediction. 2) Modern components like large-kernel depthwise convolutions and inverted bottlenecks for a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off. 3) A prompt encoder and mask decoder architecture identical to SAM to isolate backbone performance. We will pre-train the backbone on the SA-1B dataset using a masked image modeling objective adapted for convolutional networks, then fine-tune and benchmark on a suite of downstream segmentation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a strong, efficient, and scalable convolutional alternative for foundational segmentation models, challenging the dominance of ViTs in this space. It will deliver a new state-of-the-art convolutional backbone specifically optimized for segmentation, with detailed analysis of its efficiency, scaling behavior, and transfer performance compared to transformer-based counterparts.
proposals.novelty: While ConvNeXt showed CNNs can compete with ViTs on classification, its adaptation as a large-scale, pre-trained backbone for promptable segmentation is unexplored. This proposal systematically combines modern convolutional design with SAM's segmentation-specific framework, moving beyond simple backbone swaps to co-design the architecture and pre-training for the segmentation task.

proposals.title: Video-AM: Extending Segment Anything to Promptable, Temporally Consistent Video Segmentation
proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) established a powerful paradigm for promptable image segmentation, but video understanding requires temporal consistency and efficient object tracking across frames. Extending SAM's capabilities to video is a critical next step for applications in video editing, autonomous systems, and interactive video analysis. Current video object segmentation methods often lack SAM's flexibility and generalizability, creating a significant gap in promptable, foundation-level video perception.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating lightweight temporal reasoning modules into SAM's architecture and leveraging its promptable mask decoder, we can achieve robust, interactive video segmentation without sacrificing its core generalization capabilities. The key is to maintain SAM's strong spatial prior while learning to associate masks across time.
proposals.method: We propose to augment the SAM encoder with a lightweight, convolutional temporal aggregation module (inspired by 'Early Convolutions Help Transformers See Better') to compute spatio-temporal features. For tracking, we will develop a prompt propagation network that uses optical flow and cross-attention to propagate user-provided points or boxes from a reference frame to subsequent frames. The propagated prompts will then be fed into SAM's mask decoder, ensuring output masks are both accurate and temporally stable. We will train and benchmark this model, termed Video-AM, on established video segmentation datasets (e.g., DAVIS, YouTube-VOS) using a combination of contrastive tracking and segmentation losses.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first promptable foundation model for video segmentation, enabling interactive video editing and analysis with the same ease as SAM for images. It will provide a new benchmark for video object segmentation (VOS) in the era of foundation models and offer insights into integrating temporal reasoning with large-scale pre-trained vision models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the massive, static-image-pretrained SAM for dynamic video via minimal, task-specific temporal modules, preserving its generalization while adding tracking. This contrasts with prior VOS methods that are trained from scratch on video data and lack SAM's promptable interface and scale.

proposals.title: SAM-GPT: A Foundational Model for Open-World, Language-Grounded Segmentation
