proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) established a powerful promptable foundation for segmentation but lacks semantic understanding, limiting its application to tasks requiring reasoning about object identity, attributes, or context. Conversely, large language models (LLMs) possess rich world knowledge and reasoning capabilities but lack direct, fine-grained perceptual grounding in visual scenes. Bridging this gap is critical for building next-generation vision systems capable of complex, instruction-driven scene understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tight, bidirectional integration of SAM's segmentation engine with a frozen, large language model will create a unified model capable of performing open-world, language-guided segmentation and generating rich, segmentation-grounded textual descriptions, surpassing the capabilities of either component alone.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-encoder, cross-attention architecture. A visual encoder (MAE-pretrained ViT) processes the image, and its features interact with SAM's mask decoder via cross-attention to infuse semantic cues. The language features from a frozen LLM (e.g., LLaMA) are projected and fused with the visual-semantic features at multiple stages. For training, we will create a multi-task objective combining: 1) mask prediction for language queries (referring expression segmentation), 2) masked region captioning, and 3) visual question answering requiring segmentation as an intermediate step, using a blend of existing datasets (e.g., RefCOCO, Visual Genome, GRIT) and scalable web-crawled image-text-mask data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a single, end-to-end model that unifies segmentation and language understanding, enabling new capabilities like segmenting objects based on complex linguistic descriptions (e.g., 'the dog chasing the frisbee') and explaining visual scenes with object-level precision. It will provide a new benchmark for multimodal reasoning where precise spatial grounding is essential.
proposals.novelty: While prior work connects vision and language backbones, our approach is novel in its deep, architectural integration of a foundational segmentation model (SAM) with an LLM, moving beyond simple concatenation of features to enable true, bidirectional modulation of segmentation by language and vice-versa.

proposals.title: SAM-3D: A Promptable Foundation Model for 3D Scene Segmentation
proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) established a new paradigm for interactive, promptable 2D segmentation, but 3D scene understanding (e.g., for robotics, AR/VR, and autonomous systems) remains fragmented into task-specific models. A unified, promptable model for 3D data would dramatically lower the annotation burden and enable flexible interaction with 3D environments, a critical step toward general 3D perception. Current 3D segmentation methods lack this foundational, prompt-driven generality.
proposals.hypothesis: The core design principles of SAM—a promptable architecture, a massive diverse dataset, and an ambiguity-aware output—can be successfully adapted to 3D, leading to a foundational model that generalizes zero-shot to diverse 3D segmentation tasks across point clouds and volumetric grids.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SAM architecture to 3D by replacing its ViT image encoder with a hybrid 3D encoder, combining early 3D convolutions (inspired by 'Early Convolutions Help Transformers See Better') for local feature extraction with a sparse 3D transformer backbone for global context. We will curate a large-scale, diverse dataset of 3D scenes (mixing synthetic CAD models, LiDAR scans, and medical volumes) and train the model using a 3D mask decoder that accepts 3D point, box, or text prompts. The training objective will be a 3D extension of SAM's promptable mask prediction task.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first general-purpose, promptable segmentation model for 3D data, capable of zero-shot transfer to unseen 3D domains and tasks. We will release a large-scale curated 3D segmentation dataset and a benchmark to evaluate 3D foundational model capabilities, setting a new direction for 3D perception research.
proposals.novelty: While SAM is 2D-specific, directly translating its promptable paradigm to 3D is non-trivial due to the curse of dimensionality, data scarcity, and the need for efficient 3D architectures. Our approach uniquely focuses on the *foundational* and *interactive* aspects of 3D segmentation, rather than a single dataset's performance, which is a significant departure from pre-2026 3D segmentation work.

proposals.title: Hierarchical and Overlapping Segment Proposals for Holistic Scene Parsing
proposals.motivation: Current segmentation paradigms, including the Segment Anything Model (SAM), enforce a strict non-overlapping requirement for segments, treating objects as isolated entities. This is a fundamental limitation for complex scene understanding, where objects naturally exist in hierarchical relationships (e.g., a wheel on a car) and can be part of multiple semantic groups. A model that can propose overlapping and hierarchically structured segments would better reflect the compositional nature of visual scenes, enabling more powerful and interpretable visual reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a segmentation model trained to explicitly predict hierarchical and overlapping segment proposals, without a non-overlap constraint, will achieve superior performance on holistic scene parsing tasks that require reasoning about object parts, groups, and scenes as a whole.
proposals.method: We will extend the SAM architecture by modifying its output space and training objective. Instead of producing a flat set of non-overlapping masks, we will design a network head that outputs a directed graph of segments, where edges denote containment or overlap relationships. This will leverage a transformer-based decoder to iteratively refine segment masks and their relational links, using a composite loss function combining mask quality (Dice/Focal) and graph structure consistency. Training will utilize a combination of existing instance segmentation datasets (COCO, LVIS) augmented with hierarchical annotations derived from scene graphs or part hierarchies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel segmentation framework that outputs structured, overlapping segment proposals, moving beyond flat segmentation maps; 2) a new benchmark and metrics for evaluating hierarchical scene parsing; and 3) insights into how architectural inductive biases (e.g., from convolutions and transformers) can be combined for relational visual prediction, building on recent findings from 'Early Convolutions Help Transformers See Better'.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored hierarchical segmentation or scene graphs separately, the novelty lies in integrating them into a single, end-to-end trainable proposal generation system that relaxes the core non-overlap constraint of modern foundation segmentation models like SAM.

proposals.title: Unified Feature Sharing for Segmentation: A Multi-Task Architecture for Semantic and Instance Tasks
proposals.motivation: Semantic and instance segmentation are core, related vision tasks, yet they are often tackled by separate, specialized models, leading to computational redundancy and missed opportunities for synergistic learning. Recent foundational models like Segment Anything (SAM) demonstrate the power of unified architectures for segmentation prompts but are not optimized for joint semantic-instance learning. A multi-task framework that efficiently shares features could improve accuracy, reduce parameters, and enable more generalizable visual representations.
proposals.hypothesis: A carefully designed multi-task learning architecture, with a shared backbone and task-specific decoders that interact through cross-attention, can achieve superior performance on both semantic and instance segmentation compared to training separate models, while being more parameter-efficient.
proposals.method: We will design a transformer-based encoder-decoder architecture. A shared, powerful backbone (e.g., a Vision Transformer or a ConvNeXt variant) will extract a common feature pyramid. Two parallel decoders—one for semantic segmentation and one for instance segmentation (e.g., based on Mask R-CNN or CondInst heads)—will process these features. The core innovation is a bidirectional cross-attention module between the decoder layers of each task, allowing semantic context to refine instance masks and instance boundaries to sharpen semantic predictions. Training will use a balanced multi-task loss on datasets like COCO and ADE20K.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, efficient architecture for joint semantic and instance segmentation, setting a strong benchmark for multi-task segmentation performance. It will provide insights into effective feature sharing mechanisms between dense prediction tasks and release a scalable model that reduces the need for multiple standalone networks in practical applications.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning exists, applying it to unify semantic and instance segmentation with interactive, attention-based decoders is novel. This moves beyond simple shared backbones or late-fusion models to enable mid-level feature negotiation, directly addressing the tension between categorical and object-level reasoning.

proposals.title: Curriculum-Conditioned Feature Aggregation for Robust Dense Object Detection
proposals.motivation: Modern dense object detectors rely on multi-scale feature fusion, but the standard practice of fixed or uniformly weighted aggregation (e.g., in FPN or PANet) is suboptimal. This static fusion may amplify noise from certain scales during early training, leading to instability, especially when scaling to complex datasets or novel architectures like Vision Transformers. A dynamic, learning-based fusion mechanism is needed, but naively making it adaptive from the start risks learning brittle or noisy combination rules.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly conditioning a learnable feature aggregation module on a curriculum learning schedule—where the curriculum dictates the complexity of the fusion function—will stabilize training and improve final detection accuracy by first establishing robust foundational features before learning sophisticated, discriminative combinations.
proposals.method: We will design a gated aggregation network that replaces fixed FPN/PANet fusion. Its core is a small MLP or attention-based controller whose parameters are modulated by a curriculum progress signal (e.g., a function of training epoch or data difficulty). Initially, this controller is constrained to produce simple, weighted-sum-like fusion. As the curriculum advances, the controller is allowed to learn more complex, non-linear, and potentially input-dependent fusion functions. We will integrate this into a Mask R-CNN or DETR-style framework, using a simple image- or loss-based curriculum (e.g., from 'Segment Anything' data) to schedule the controller's capacity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new paradigm for jointly learning network architecture and training dynamics, moving beyond static fusion designs. It will deliver a practical, plug-in module for dense detection that improves training stability and final performance on benchmarks like COCO and LVIS, particularly for models struggling with scale variation and cluttered scenes.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning typically sequences data, here it directly governs an architectural module's internal function. The synergy between a dynamically evolving training stage and a correspondingly adaptive network fusion mechanism is a novel direction for model design.

proposals.title: JointNAS: Co-Searching Architectures and Adaptive Focal Losses for Dense Prediction
proposals.motivation: Dense prediction tasks like instance segmentation are plagued by extreme foreground-background imbalance and varying pixel difficulty. While Focal Loss is a fixed heuristic for this, its static parameters are suboptimal across different network depths and datasets. Neural Architecture Search (NAS) has advanced model design but remains decoupled from the loss function, a critical component influencing gradient flow and what the model learns to emphasize.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly searching over a model's architectural components and the parameterization of a spatially- and statistically-aware focal loss function will yield superior task-adaptive models, where the architecture and the loss are co-optimized to address data imbalance more effectively than sequential design.
proposals.method: We will develop a differentiable NAS framework (e.g., based on DARTS) where the search space includes both architectural primitives (e.g., convolution types, layer cardinality) and parameters of a generalized focal loss. The loss will be modeled as a lightweight network module that takes spatial feature statistics as input and outputs modulating parameters (gamma, alpha) per feature level or region. The controller will optimize architecture weights and loss parameters end-to-end on target tasks (e.g., COCO instance segmentation) using a bi-level optimization strategy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel NAS framework that unifies architecture and loss function search, 2) new insights into how loss adaptation interacts with architectural choice for handling data imbalance, and 3) state-of-the-art, compact models for instance segmentation that are inherently robust by design.
proposals.novelty: This is the first work to integrate parameterized, adaptive loss functions directly into the NAS search space, moving beyond the paradigm of searching architectures under a fixed, hand-designed loss. The co-adaptation of structure and objective is a non-obvious but promising direction for automated machine learning.

proposals.title: Multi-Scale ResNeXt: An Explicit Multi-Path Architecture for Semantic and Instance Segmentation
proposals.motivation: ResNeXt's grouped convolution design excels at classification by efficiently widening networks, but its application to dense prediction tasks like segmentation remains underexplored. Segmentation requires capturing both broad context for semantic regions and fine details for object boundaries, a challenge where standard multi-scale approaches like FPN or ASPP are applied post-hoc on backbone features. A core architectural redesign, inspired by multi-domain signal analysis, could more effectively unify these scale-specific needs within a single, efficient network framework.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly specializing groups of parallel transformation paths within a ResNeXt block to operate on different effective receptive fields—analogous to a wavelet filter bank—we can create a backbone inherently more powerful for segmentation, improving both semantic coherence and instance boundary accuracy.
proposals.method: We will architecturally modify the ResNeXt block, creating a 'Multi-Scale ResNeXt' (MS-ResNeXt) block. Within each block, the cardinality (number of grouped paths) will be divided into subsets, each with a distinct convolutional kernel size (e.g., 1x1, 3x3, 5x5) and potentially different dilation rates. These parallel paths, processing the same input feature map, will be specialized to capture fine, medium, and coarse spatial patterns. Their outputs will be aggregated via concatenation or summation within the residual structure. This design will be integrated into standard segmentation heads (e.g., Mask R-CNN for instance, FCN for semantic) and evaluated on COCO and ADE20K.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scale-specialized backbone architecture that extends the ResNeXt design philosophy to dense prediction. It will provide a systematic evaluation of ResNeXt for modern segmentation tasks and establish a new architectural paradigm for building multi-scale understanding directly into network blocks, rather than relying solely on neck modules.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, fixed assignment of scale-specific operations to different grouped paths within the ResNeXt block itself, creating an intrinsic multi-scale filter bank. This differs from prior multi-scale methods that apply transformations sequentially or in parallel on already-computed features from a homogeneous backbone.

proposals.title: Inception-SAM: A Split-Transform-Merge Architecture for Efficient and Generalizable Segmentation
proposals.motivation: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) demonstrates remarkable zero-shot segmentation capability but is computationally intensive, limiting its real-time application. Concurrently, modern vision architectures like Vision Transformers (ViTs) and ConvNeXts have moved towards simpler, homogeneous designs, potentially overlooking the proven efficacy of multi-path, multi-scale feature processing exemplified by the Inception network's split-transform-merge paradigm. Revisiting this paradigm within contemporary scaling and design principles could yield more parameter- and compute-efficient foundational models for segmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically integrating a modernized split-transform-merge paradigm into the core building blocks of a segmentation foundation model will significantly improve its computational efficiency and scaling behavior without sacrificing, and potentially enhancing, its generalization performance on diverse segmentation tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a new model family, Inception-SAM, by replacing the standard transformer blocks in the SAM image encoder with novel 'Meta-Inception' blocks. Each block will implement a parallel multi-branch structure: one branch will use a depthwise convolution for local context, another a small-window self-attention for mid-range dependencies, and a third a channel-wise MLP for global mixing. A dynamic, learned gating mechanism (inspired by Mixture-of-Experts) will perform the 'merge' step, weighting branch outputs conditioned on the input. We will benchmark scaling laws by varying model depth, width, and the number of parallel branches, evaluating on the SA-1B dataset and downstream transfer tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, efficient architecture for segmentation that explicitly encodes multi-scale inductive bias. It will provide a rigorous analysis of scaling laws for heterogeneous, multi-path networks compared to homogeneous counterparts. The resulting public models and code will enable more deployable foundational segmentation.
proposals.novelty: While Inception is a classic concept, its principled re-design and integration into a large-scale, transformer-dominated segmentation foundation model is novel. The proposed dynamic gating mechanism for merging branches moves beyond simple concatenation or addition, adapting the computational graph to the input.

proposals.title: Cardinality Scaling in 3D CNNs: A Unified Framework for Efficient Video Understanding
proposals.motivation: Video understanding models are computationally prohibitive, limiting their scalability and accessibility. While the cardinality of grouped convolutions in 2D ResNeXt networks proved highly effective for image tasks, its principles remain underexplored for 3D convolutional networks (3D CNNs) which are fundamental for video analysis. A systematic study of scaling dimensions—width, depth, and cardinality—in 3D CNNs is needed to establish efficient and performant design principles for video tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that increasing the cardinality (number of independent groups) in 3D convolutional blocks is a more effective scaling strategy for video recognition than increasing width or depth alone, leading to better accuracy/compute trade-offs on large-scale video datasets.
proposals.method: We will design and benchmark a family of 3D ResNeXt architectures, scaling them along width, depth, and cardinality axes on Kinetics-400/600. We will adapt the grouped convolution design from 2D ResNeXt, implementing efficient 3D grouped convolutions. The core experiment will involve extensive ablation studies to isolate the effect of cardinality, followed by training the optimal models end-to-end and evaluating on video action recognition, temporal segmentation, and potentially video object detection tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive analysis of cardinality scaling for 3D CNNs, delivering a new set of efficient model families for video analysis. It will establish clear design guidelines for scaling video architectures, potentially leading to state-of-the-art results on major benchmarks with improved computational efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While 3D CNNs and the ResNeXt concept are known, their intersection is unexplored; this work systematically translates and validates the cardinality scaling principle from 2D to the spatiotemporal domain, which is non-trivial due to the added temporal dimension and computational complexity.

proposals.title: Beyond Object Categories: A Foundation Model for Segmenting Parts, Materials, and States
proposals.motivation: Current segmentation models, including foundation models like SAM, are primarily designed for whole-object or instance segmentation, treating categories as atomic units. This limits their utility for detailed scene understanding, which requires reasoning about object parts (e.g., a car's wheel), materials (e.g., fabric, metal), and transient states (e.g., broken, wet). Advancing beyond entry-level categories is crucial for applications in robotics, augmented reality, and detailed visual reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, unified segmentation model can be trained to segment visual concepts at multiple granularities—objects, their constituent parts, materials, and states—by leveraging a structured, hierarchical prompt space and a scalable, transformer-based architecture.
proposals.method: We will extend the Segment Anything Model (SAM) architecture and training methodology. The core technical approach involves: 1) Curating and generating a large-scale dataset with hierarchical annotations for objects, parts, materials, and states, building upon datasets like ADE20K, PartImageNet, and OpenSurfaces. 2) Designing a multi-granularity prompt encoder that accepts textual or visual queries specifying the desired concept level (e.g., 'segment the metallic parts'). 3) Training a modified Mask Decoder with auxiliary heads to predict concept-type labels alongside masks, using a loss that balances mask quality across granularities.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new segmentation foundation model that breaks the atomic object category paradigm. It will provide the community with a powerful tool for fine-grained image understanding and a new benchmark for evaluating segmentation across conceptual hierarchies. The model and dataset will catalyze research in detailed scene parsing and vision-language reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work segments objects or specific attributes in isolation, this proposal aims for a single, promptable model that dynamically segments across fundamentally different visual concept types (parts, materials, states) without being constrained to a fixed, flat category ontology.

proposals.title: Consensus-Driven Refinement: A Framework for Scalable, High-Quality Segmentation Annotation
proposals.motivation: The success of large-scale segmentation models like Segment Anything is fundamentally limited by the quality and consistency of their training data. Current annotation pipelines for segmentation are costly, prone to noise, and suffer from inter-annotator disagreement, creating a bottleneck for model performance and generalization. Addressing this data quality bottleneck is a critical next step for advancing segmentation research.
proposals.hypothesis: A model-in-the-loop annotation system that explicitly models and resolves annotator disagreement through iterative consensus-building can produce segmentation masks of higher quality and consistency than standard protocols, thereby improving downstream model performance.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, we adapt the Segment Anything Model (SAM) as a strong prior to generate initial mask proposals for a given image. Second, we design a lightweight transformer-based refinement network that takes multiple, potentially noisy human annotations for the same object as input. This network, inspired by architectures that blend convolutions and transformers for robust feature learning, will learn to predict a consensus mask and an uncertainty map, highlighting regions of annotator disagreement. The system will be deployed in an active learning loop where uncertain regions are prioritized for re-annotation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable protocol for collecting high-fidelity segmentation data. We will release a benchmark dataset with consensus-refined annotations and demonstrate through controlled experiments on detection/segmentation transfer learning tasks that models trained on our refined data achieve superior performance and robustness compared to those trained on standard annotations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses models to assist annotation, our approach uniquely focuses on explicitly modeling the *process* of annotator disagreement to algorithmically derive a consensus ground truth, moving beyond simple aggregation or adjudication. The tight integration of a refinement network trained to resolve conflicts is a novel technical direction.

proposals.title: Scaling Entry-Level Recognition: A Data-Centric Framework for Expanding to 10,000 Object Categories
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale recognition systems, like those trained on ImageNet-1K, are limited to a constrained vocabulary of ~1,000 entry-level categories, which restricts their utility for real-world applications requiring fine-grained knowledge. The success of foundational models in NLP, trained on web-scale data, suggests that scaling visual category vocabularies is a critical next step for computer vision. This work addresses the fundamental data, modeling, and evaluation challenges inherent in scaling category recognition by an order of magnitude.
proposals.hypothesis: A systematic, data-centric approach combining web-scale weak supervision with a carefully designed training and evaluation protocol can enable robust recognition across an order-of-magnitude more entry-level object categories without a proportional drop in per-category accuracy.
proposals.method: We will construct a new dataset, 'ImageNet-10K', by programmatically expanding the WordNet hierarchy and leveraging large-scale web image-text pairs (e.g., from LAION) for weak supervision, followed by human verification of a core subset. The modeling approach will build on the ConvNeXt and hybrid Conv-Transformer architectures, known for strong scaling behavior. We will employ a staged training recipe, starting with web-supervised pre-training followed by fine-tuning on verified data, and introduce a new evaluation benchmark with tiered difficulty to measure scaling laws and granularity effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a scalable methodology and a new benchmark for large-vocabulary recognition, 2) a set of pre-trained models for 10,000 categories that serve as a stronger foundation for downstream vision tasks, and 3) an analysis of the scaling laws and failure modes associated with expanding the semantic vocabulary of vision models.
proposals.novelty: While scaling model size and data is well-studied, systematically scaling the *semantic breadth* of recognition systems—the number of distinct categories—in a data-efficient and evaluable way remains an open challenge. Our work uniquely focuses on the data curation and architectural co-design needed for this specific form of scaling, moving beyond simply adding more images to existing categories.

proposals.title: Scaling and Design Principles for Long-Tail Instance Segmentation: A Comprehensive LVIS Benchmark Study
proposals.motivation: The LVIS dataset presents a critical challenge in long-tail instance segmentation, where rare categories are severely underrepresented. As of 2025, progress on LVIS is fragmented, with studies often focusing on narrow architectural improvements or specialized loss functions without a unified analysis of scaling laws and foundational design choices. A systematic benchmark is needed to disentangle the effects of model capacity, data scaling, and architectural inductive biases on long-tail performance, guiding future research and practical system design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic scaling of model and data size, combined with specific architectural designs that bridge convolutional and transformer backbones, will yield predictable and substantial gains on LVIS, and that the long-tail performance bottleneck is more a function of insufficient representation learning for rare classes than an inherent architectural limitation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, controlled empirical study on LVIS v1.0. The core method involves training and evaluating a suite of models, including Mask R-CNN and Cascade Mask R-CNN frameworks, with diverse backbones (ResNeXt, ConvNeXt, Swin Transformers, Hybrid designs like CoAtNet). We will systematically vary scaling dimensions: model size (width/depth), training data scale (using self-training with pseudo-labels from models like Segment Anything), and training schedule length. All experiments will use a standardized training protocol (e.g., Detectron2) to isolate variable effects. Performance will be tracked per category frequency group (rare, common, frequent).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a clear, reproducible benchmark of state-of-the-art methods on LVIS, establishing new baselines. It will yield concrete design guidelines and scaling laws for long-tail segmentation, identifying which factors (architecture, data, compute) most efficiently improve rare-class recall. The released code, configs, and model zoo will serve as a standard reference for future research.
proposals.novelty: While individual methods have been tested on LVIS, a holistic study that jointly analyzes the interacting effects of backbone architecture, model/data scaling, and training strategy specifically for long-tail segmentation is novel. It moves beyond reporting incremental gains to establishing principled, predictive trends that explain progress.

proposals.title: Active Annotation for Segmentation: Scaling Data Curation with Model-Driven Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Foundation models like Segment Anything (SAM) have shifted the bottleneck in computer vision from model design to data curation. Annotating segmentation masks at scale is prohibitively expensive and time-consuming. Active learning, which strategically selects data for annotation, offers a principled path to maximize annotation efficiency, directly addressing a core challenge in scaling segmentation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active learning framework, leveraging the uncertainty and representativeness of predictions from a segmentation model, can identify the most informative images for annotation, thereby achieving state-of-the-art segmentation performance with a significantly reduced annotation budget.
proposals.method: We will develop a pool-based active learning loop for instance segmentation. Starting with a small seed set, we will use a model (e.g., a Mask R-CNN or a ViT-Det backbone) to infer masks on unlabeled images. We will prioritize images using a composite acquisition function combining predictive uncertainty (e.g., entropy over mask probabilities) and diversity (e.g., core-set selection or features from an early convolutional stage). Selected images will be annotated via the SAM-assisted annotation protocol, the model will be fine-tuned, and the loop will iterate. We will benchmark on COCO and LVIS.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous benchmark for active learning in modern segmentation tasks, demonstrating a clear annotation-performance trade-off curve. It will deliver a practical, open-source framework for efficient dataset expansion, lowering the barrier to creating high-quality segmentation datasets for new domains.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is classic, its application is novel in the context of modern, large-scale segmentation models like SAM and vision transformers, where the acquisition function must account for complex, instance-level predictions and long-tailed category distributions.

proposals.title: Mask2Cap: Recovering Semantic Information from Instance Segmentation Masks
proposals.motivation: The field of segmentation, exemplified by models like Segment Anything, has focused on generating masks from prompts or images. However, the inverse problem—reasoning about an object's identity or attributes from its mask alone—is underexplored and fundamentally challenging. Solving it would enable new applications in data augmentation, synthetic data analysis, and understanding what visual information is encoded purely in shape and location, independent of texture and color.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an instance segmentation mask, despite lacking photometric detail, contains sufficient structural and contextual information (e.g., shape, size, relative position) for a neural network to generate accurate descriptive captions or category predictions.
proposals.method: We will design a transformer-based encoder-decoder network. The encoder will process the binary mask and optional positional metadata (e.g., bounding box) using a combination of convolutional layers (inspired by 'Early Convolutions Help Transformers See Better') to extract hierarchical shape features, followed by a transformer to model spatial relationships. The decoder will be a causal language model, trained via a cross-entropy loss to generate textual descriptions. We will create a large-scale training dataset by pairing masks from COCO, LVIS, and SA-1B with their ground-truth captions and category labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel task and benchmark for 'mask-to-language' reasoning. It will provide insights into the semantic content carried by shape and its sufficiency for recognition, complementing the literature on image-to-text generation. The resulting model could serve as a core component for analyzing and annotating mask datasets without original imagery.
proposals.novelty: While captioning from images is well-studied, generating descriptions from a mask alone is a distinct, more constrained problem that isolates shape-based reasoning. This inverts the standard segmentation paradigm and probes the information bottleneck of mask representations.

proposals.title: Few-Pixel Instance Segmentation: A Hybrid Architecture and Scale-Aware Training Benchmark
proposals.motivation: Instance segmentation models, including foundation models like Segment Anything, struggle with objects represented by only a handful of pixels, which are common in aerial imagery, medical microscopy, and wide-field surveillance. This failure limits their application in critical domains where small objects are of primary interest. Current benchmarks lack a systematic focus on this extreme low-resolution regime, making progress difficult to measure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid architecture combining the inductive biases of early-stage convolutions with the contextual power of transformers, trained with a scale-aware objective, will significantly outperform existing monolithic models on few-pixel instance segmentation.
proposals.method: We will design a 'Conv-Transformer' encoder where initial convolutional blocks process high-resolution feature maps to preserve fine spatial details, feeding into a transformer for global reasoning. Inspired by 'Early Convolutions Help Transformers See Better', we will optimize this hybrid design specifically for small objects. A novel 'Multi-Scale Focal Loss' will be developed, dynamically weighting loss based on instance pixel area to focus learning on small instances. We will create a rigorous benchmark by curating and annotating the small-object subsets of existing datasets like COCO and LVIS.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a new state-of-the-art model for small-instance segmentation, 2) a publicly available benchmark for few-pixel segmentation to guide future research, and 3) insights into the architectural and optimization requirements for extreme scale variance in segmentation tasks.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid architectures exist, none are explicitly designed and trained from the ground up for the few-pixel regime. The proposed scale-aware loss and dedicated benchmark move beyond simply evaluating on small objects to fundamentally re-prioritizing them during both model design and training.

ideas.title: Develop models for full scene understanding
ideas.description: Create models that go beyond object recognition to understand scene context and relationships.
ideas.sourceTitle: Microsoft COCO: Common Objects in Context

ideas.title: Curriculum-Driven Architectural Scaling for Dense Detection
ideas.description: This research proposes to unify curriculum learning with the systematic study of architectural scaling laws for dense object detection. The core idea is to not only sequence training data from easy to hard examples, but to simultaneously and strategically evolve the network's architecture—specifically the interplay of its cardinality (number of parallel branches), depth, and width—in sync with this curriculum. This creates a 'growing' or 'adaptive capacity' model where architectural complexity is tailored to the current learning phase, potentially starting with simpler, more efficient configurations for easy data and progressively scaling specific dimensions to tackle harder examples. The synergy yields a novel understanding of how scaling laws are not static but are dependent on the data distribution and learning stage, leading to more sample-efficient training and potentially uncovering optimal, non-uniform scaling pathways for dense visual recognition tasks.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Guided Adaptive Aggregation for Dense Object Detection
ideas.description: This research direction investigates a novel training paradigm for dense detection models where the standard sum or fixed aggregation of features from different scales or heads is replaced with a learnable, adaptive aggregation module. The key innovation is that the behavior of this aggregation module is explicitly conditioned on the current stage of a curriculum learning schedule, starting with simpler, more robust aggregation for easy examples and progressively introducing more complex, discriminative combination functions for harder cases. This allows the model to first solidify fundamental detection capabilities before learning to leverage sophisticated, potentially noisier feature interactions, leading to more stable training and improved final performance on complex scenes. The synergy lies in using the curriculum not just to sequence data, but to actively shape the model's internal architecture and fusion logic throughout the learning process.

ideas.title: Neural Architecture Search for Task-Adaptive Focal Loss Functions in Dense Prediction
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel Neural Architecture Search (NAS) framework that jointly optimizes the model architecture (including layer cardinality) and a parameterized, adaptive loss function for dense prediction tasks like instance segmentation. Instead of using a fixed Focal Loss, the NAS controller searches for optimal parameters (e.g., focusing gamma, alpha, or even spatial modulation) for a generalized focal loss function that is specific to different stages of the network and the statistical properties of the task's data. This allows the model to automatically discover architectural configurations whose learning is guided by a bespoke, imbalance-aware loss, leading to more robust feature learning where challenging foreground pixels receive dynamically calibrated attention throughout the network hierarchy. The core synergy is the closed-loop optimization where the architecture informs the loss function's adaptation and the adaptive loss, in turn, shapes the search for a more effective architecture.

ideas.title: Self-Paced Curriculum Learning for Dense Detection via Online Difficulty Measurement
ideas.description: This approach implements an adaptive curriculum for dense object detection where the 'difficulty' of a training example is not pre-defined but is continuously estimated online based on the model's current performance. A meta-controller module analyzes metrics like the per-instance loss decay rate or predictive uncertainty to dynamically rank and sample batches from a pool of training data. The system prioritizes examples that are neither too easy (negligible learning signal) nor too hard (chaotic gradients), thereby maintaining a high learning pace throughout training. This results in more robust convergence and final performance by ensuring the model's training signal is consistently informative.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Scale Analysis from Seismology for ResNeXt in Segmentation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from seismology's use of multi-scale wavelet transforms to analyze earthquakes, this idea proposes to solve the challenge of applying ResNeXt to semantic and instance segmentation. In seismology, signals are decomposed to separately study large-scale tectonics and fine-scale fractures. Adapted to computer vision, the solution is to architecturally modify the ResNeXt network, creating a 'Multi-Scale ResNeXt' where groups of parallel transform paths are specialized for different effective receptive fields and feature scales. This explicit multi-scale design, integrated within the residual grouping framework, would allow a single network to more effectively capture the broad contextual information needed for semantic coherence and the fine-grained details required for instance-level boundaries, thereby extending ResNeXt's evaluation and performance on segmentation benchmarks.

ideas.title: Adaptive Multi-Scale Sensing for Efficient Object Detection
ideas.description: This research challenges the core assumption in one-stage object detection that dense, uniform sampling of anchor boxes or feature points is always necessary for high accuracy. Drawing an analogy from seismic imaging, where hybrid sensor deployment optimizes cost and detail, we propose a novel adaptive multi-scale sensing framework. A fast, preliminary network first analyzes the input image to create a 'complexity heatmap,' identifying regions likely containing small, occluded, or densely packed objects. The main detection network then uses this heatmap to dynamically allocate its computational budget, applying dense, fine-grained sampling only to these high-complexity regions while employing a much sparser sampling strategy in simpler, homogeneous areas of the image. This hybrid approach aims to maintain or improve detection accuracy, particularly for challenging objects, while significantly reducing the computational cost and redundancy associated with processing every location with equal intensity.

ideas.title: Adversarial Hard Example Synthesis via Focal Loss Gradient Ascent
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational chemistry, where generative models perform gradient ascent in a latent space to create molecules that maximize a target property (like binding affinity). Here, the analogous property is the focal loss of a trained image classification model. The adapted research idea is to treat the trained model's focal loss as an objective function and use gradient-based optimization (e.g., gradient ascent) on the pixel values of synthetic images to generate novel, 'maximally hard' training examples. These synthesized images represent the frontier of the model's current failure modes. By iteratively generating such hard examples and retraining the model on them, we create a self-improving curriculum that directly targets scaling bottlenecks, making the model more robust and efficient to scale.

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Task-Adaptive Difficulty Weighting for Dense Prediction
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a direct application of Focal Loss by formulating the problem of handling hard examples in dense prediction as learning an optimal loss-weighting function. For a target task like instance segmentation or dense pose, we first instrument the training to extract multi-faceted difficulty signals for every pixel or region. A small meta-network then consumes these signals and dynamically outputs a per-sample, per-element weighting that modulates the base task loss. This meta-learner is trained with a bi-level optimization to minimize validation error, forcing it to discover weighting strategies that truly improve generalization, which may differ radically from the fixed Focal Loss form.

ideas.title: Dual problem: single-scale detection with FPN

ideas.title: Adapting Load Balancing from Distributed Systems for Gradient Harmonization in Object Detection
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from distributed computing, where load balancing dynamically distributes network traffic to prevent server overload. The analogous solution involves mechanisms like weighted round-robin that assign tasks based on capacity and current load. Adapted to object detection loss design, we treat gradients from imbalanced categories as an uneven computational load. The research idea is to design a dynamic gradient balancing mechanism that re-weights gradients during backpropagation based on real-time statistics (e.g., magnitude, frequency), functioning like a load balancer for the optimization process. This would automatically harmonize the influence of easy/hard and frequent/rare samples, leading to more stable and effective training for object detection models.

ideas.title: Efficient Gigapixel Detection via Learnable Sparsity Probes and Adaptive High-Resolution RoI Refinement
ideas.description: This system addresses the computational intractability of monolithic gigapixel processing by replacing the brute-force approach with an efficient, adaptive pipeline. A lightweight Sparsity Probe Network first performs a rapid, low-resolution scan of the entire image to identify candidate regions likely to contain objects. These candidate regions are then extracted and processed at high resolution by a dedicated detection network. The entire architecture is trained end-to-end, with gradients from the focal loss of the high-resolution detector guiding the probe network to improve its region proposal accuracy, creating a computationally feasible and highly targeted system for ultra-high-resolution, sparse object detection.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sample Hardening via Boosting for Object Detector Training
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational epidemiology, where disease models are iteratively improved by focusing simulation resources on high-risk, hard-to-predict outbreak scenarios. The analogous solution is applied to detector training by establishing a boosting framework that iteratively re-weights the training process. After each training epoch or stage, the system evaluates the detector's errors, identifying specific object classes, scales, occlusions, or background contexts that are poorly handled. These 'high-risk' examples or regions are then given increased weight in the loss function or are strategically oversampled in subsequent training batches. This creates a self-improving loop where the detector adaptively hardens itself against its own evolving failure modes, similar to how an ensemble booster builds a strong learner from weak ones, but applied internally within a single detector's optimization trajectory.

ideas.title: Cardinality-Aware Hard Example Synthesis for Theoretical Validation and Model Fortification
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the generation of hard examples with the theoretical analysis of cardinality. We propose using a trained model's focal loss (or related sensitivity metrics) not just to find hard examples, but to synthesize novel, maximally challenging images that are constrained by a theoretical model of how cardinality (the size of a transformation set) organizes the feature space. The synthesized examples then serve as targeted probes to validate, challenge, and refine the theoretical framework. This creates a synergistic loop where theory guides hard example generation to expose specific architectural weaknesses, and the empirical results from these adversarial examples feed back to improve the theoretical understanding of why cardinality works.

ideas.title: Efficient Long-Tail Recognition for Edge Devices via Adaptive ResNeXt and Dynamic Focal Loss
ideas.description: This research direction designs a novel, efficient variant of the ResNeXt architecture specifically optimized for mobile and embedded devices, with the primary objective of tackling extremely long-tailed, real-world datasets (e.g., hundreds of rare wildlife species or fine-grained industrial defects). The key innovation is the co-design of the model's grouped convolution structure and a dynamic Focal Loss mechanism, where the loss's focusing parameter is automatically adjusted not only based on class frequency but also on the current mini-batch statistics and a target latency/energy budget on the device. This synergy enables a single, compact model to maintain high accuracy on both head and tail classes while strictly adhering to the computational constraints of edge deployment, a challenge neither efficient architecture design nor class-imbalance loss techniques address in isolation.

ideas.title: Lightweight RetinaNet with Cross-Path Attention for Mobile Detection
ideas.description: This research proposes novel, extremely lightweight RetinaNet variants designed for mobile and edge deployment. The core innovation replaces or augments standard convolutional blocks with multi-branch structures where each branch processes features at a different scale or abstraction. Crucially, lightweight cross-path attention or gating mechanisms are introduced to enable selective communication between these parallel paths within a block. This allows the network to dynamically integrate complementary contextual information from different pathways, significantly boosting detection accuracy for its parameter and FLOP budget, without relying on heavy standard operations.

ideas.title: Apply Progressive Muscle Loading to Dense Detection Training
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of progressive overload from Sports Science and Physical Rehabilitation to dense object detection. In that field, training begins with foundational, low-intensity exercises before systematically increasing load and complexity to build strength and prevent injury. The adapted solution structures the detector's training curriculum to start on easy, synthetic scenes with few, large, canonical objects. The 'load' is then progressively increased by sequentially introducing harder challenges: more objects, smaller scales, occlusion, cluttered backgrounds, and finally real-world noise. This multi-axis progression allows the network to develop robust detection capabilities in a stable, graduated manner, mirroring how athletes build performance capacity.

ideas.title: Gradient-Coordinated Multi-Task Dense Prediction via Dynamic Conflict Resolution
ideas.description: This redesigned system jointly optimizes detection, segmentation, and depth prediction by dynamically managing inter-task gradient interactions. It employs a real-time gradient coordination layer that analyzes task-specific gradient magnitudes and directional conflicts during training. Based on this analysis, it adaptively re-weights task losses or applies projection techniques to align gradients, preventing any single task from dominating. This approach ensures balanced and synergistic learning across all heterogeneous dense prediction tasks, leading to more robust and higher-performing multi-task models.

ideas.title: Adapting Focal Loss Principles to Hinge Loss for Imbalanced Classification
ideas.description: This research seed explores reshaping base loss functions like hinge loss to handle class imbalance, moving beyond the common focus on cross-entropy. Drawing an analogy from signal processing—specifically perceptual audio/image compression, where non-linear perceptual models reshape the error metric to focus quantization effort on perceptually salient features—we adapt this core principle. The analogous solution uses a perceptually-weighted function to reshape uniform error. Adapted to machine learning, we propose developing a modulated hinge loss that applies a class- and margin-sensitive weighting factor. This 'Focal Hinge Loss' would dynamically reduce the influence of easy majority-class examples with large margins and increase the focus on hard or minority-class examples near the decision boundary, directly tackling the class imbalance problem for margin-based classifiers like SVMs.

ideas.title: Dynamically Aligned Loss and Sampling via Meta-Learning for Class Imbalance
ideas.description: This research proposes a unified framework that meta-learns the synergy between loss function adaptation and data sampling to address class imbalance. Instead of a static combination, a controller module observes training gradients and performance metrics to co-adapt the Focal Loss's focusing parameters and the instance sampling weights in real-time. This ensures the loss landscape and the data distribution are collaboratively optimized, preventing conflict and improving robustness. The approach is evaluated on highly imbalanced image and text classification benchmarks against static combinations.

ideas.title: Systematic Ablation Framework for Isulating Non-Imbalance Bottlenecks in One-Stage Detectors
ideas.description: This research proposes a rigorous experimental framework to identify and rank the most significant non-imbalance bottlenecks in one-stage object detectors. It defines a taxonomy of potential architectural and optimization limitations, such as feature pyramid misalignment, task-specific gradient conflicts, and sensitivity to localization noise. Using a modular detector design, the framework conducts controlled ablation studies, isolating each factor to quantify its individual impact on final accuracy. The outcome is a prioritized list of bottlenecks, providing clear targets for future architectural innovation beyond the well-studied class imbalance problem.

ideas.title: Robust Boosting for Detector Training with Outlier and Noise Resilience
ideas.description: This redesigned approach applies boosting principles to detector training but explicitly guards against overfitting to noise and outliers. It uses a small committee of models trained on different data subsets to estimate a 'noise susceptibility' score for each training example. The core boosting algorithm then adjusts sample weights based on detector errors, but the weight updates are tempered by this score, suppressing the influence of likely noise. The final system iteratively focuses on genuinely hard examples while being robust to label errors and data artifacts, leading to more reliable and generalizable detectors.

ideas.title: Leveraging High-Fidelity Data First for Model Calibration
ideas.description: This idea inverts the focus from hard to easy examples by drawing an analogy from geophysical surveying. In seismic inversion, the solution to the ill-posed problem begins by building an initial model from high-fidelity, easy-to-interpret well-log data before integrating complex, noisy seismic surveys. Adapting this, the research explores a training regime where a model is first—and periodically—trained to convergence on a curated set of 'easy' examples (high-confidence labels, simple features). This core set provides a stable foundation and a calibration point. The model then learns from progressively harder data, but periodically resets or strongly regularizes its parameters back towards performance on the easy anchor set. This is hypothesized to accelerate overall convergence by preventing divergence into poor local minima and to improve stability by mitigating catastrophic forgetting of fundamental features when confronted with ambiguous or adversarial data.

ideas.title: Dual-Phase Representation & Modulation for Extreme Long-Tailed Recognition
ideas.description: This research proposes a two-phase framework to tackle extreme long-tailed recognition beyond simple loss function adjustments. First, a representation learning phase employs contrastive or supervised pre-training on the entire imbalanced dataset to learn general, discriminative features. Second, a modulation fine-tuning phase combines Focal Loss with dynamic class-aware components, such as a trainable weight vector for each class or a balanced meta-sampler, to specifically enhance tail-class decision boundaries. The system is evaluated on datasets with hundreds of classes and natural imbalance ratios exceeding 1:1000, measuring both overall accuracy and per-class recall for tail categories.

ideas.title: Adaptive Curriculum Learning via Multi-Armed Bandit for Hard Example Mining
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from adaptive learning systems in educational technology, this idea adapts the solution of using a contextual multi-armed bandit for dynamic curriculum sequencing. The analogous problem—selecting the next optimal lesson for a student—is solved by modeling lesson types as bandit arms and using learning gain as a reward. Adapted to hard example mining for object detection, different mining strategies (e.g., highest loss, class-balanced hardest, semantic outlier) become the arms. The bandit's context is the detector's current performance profile, and the reward is the measured improvement on a validation set after training on a batch mined with the chosen strategy. This creates a computationally efficient, adaptive mining controller that explicitly balances exploring new mining strategies against exploiting the currently most effective one to maximize the detector's learning progress.

ideas.title: Biomimetic Neural Pruning for Efficient RetinaNet
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from developmental neurobiology, where organisms over-produce then selectively prune neural connections to create efficient circuits, this research adapts that solution to computer vision. We propose creating extremely lightweight RetinaNet variants for mobile/edge deployment by implementing a biomimetic neural pruning algorithm. Starting with a standard RetinaNet, the method will systematically identify and remove redundant filters, channels, and connections with minimal impact on detection accuracy, mimicking biological optimization. This results in an organically streamlined architecture that maintains critical detection pathways while drastically reducing computational cost and memory footprint, enabling real-time performance on resource-constrained devices without extensive manual architecture redesign.

ideas.title: Dynamic Architecture Adaptation via Layer-Specific Imbalanced Loss Functions
ideas.description: This research direction proposes designing neural networks where the fundamental building blocks (e.g., convolution types, attention mechanisms, activation functions) are not repeated homogeneously but are instead dynamically selected or parameterized per layer based on the evolving demands of a reshaped, imbalanced loss function. We explore co-designing layer-specific architectures with variants of base losses like hinge loss or focal loss, where the loss function itself informs which block structure is most effective for learning robust features from minority classes at that particular depth. This creates a synergistic feedback loop: the adaptive architecture mitigates the inherent learning difficulties posed by imbalance, while the tailored, non-cross-entropy loss provides a precise training signal to guide the architectural specialization across the network's hierarchy.

ideas.title: Learning Implicit Priors: A Meta-Learning Framework to Replace Static Class Balancing in Focal Loss
ideas.description: This research challenges the static alpha parameter in Focal Loss by arguing its true purpose is to align the loss function's implicit prior with the data. We design a system that meta-learns an adaptive weighting function, conditioned on dataset statistics like class frequency, label noise, and feature separability. This approach generalizes beyond simple class frequency balancing, providing a principled, data-driven replacement for the alpha term. The outcome is a more robust and theoretically grounded loss function that automatically adapts to diverse dataset characteristics without manual hyperparameter tuning.

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Contextual Aggregation via Information Bottlenecks
ideas.description: This research proposes to learn the aggregation function as a context-aware, meta-optimized module rather than selecting it from a fixed set. The system treats the set of transformation outputs as an unordered input to a neural module whose parameters are conditioned on a contextual signal from the primary task. The aggregation function is meta-trained with an objective that combines task performance with an information bottleneck, forcing it to compress the set into a minimal sufficient statistic for the downstream objective. This approach yields a principled, adaptive aggregation mechanism that theoretically generalizes better and provides interpretable criteria for its behavior based on information flow.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning Loss Functions via Bi-Level Optimization for Target Metric Alignment Under Imbalance
ideas.description: This research proposes a systematic framework for designing loss functions that are directly aligned with target evaluation metrics (e.g., F1, G-mean) in class-imbalanced settings. Instead of heuristically reshaping a single base loss, the method treats the loss function as a parameterized model, which is meta-optimized on a validation set using the target metric as the outer-loop objective. This bi-level optimization approach can adapt any differentiable base loss structure, ensuring the learned loss corrects for the training data distribution's skew. The resulting loss functions are theoretically grounded to improve performance on the metric of interest and are empirically validated across multiple imbalance ratios and dataset domains.

ideas.title: Dynamic Multi-Path Blocks with Learned Cardinality and Architecture
ideas.description: This research direction proposes neural network blocks where the internal multi-branch structure is not fixed. For each input, the block can activate a variable number of parallel transformation paths, with the cardinality determined by a lightweight, data-dependent controller. Crucially, each active path can instantiate a different architectural primitive (e.g., convolution, self-attention, or a custom micro-architecture), allowing the block to assemble a bespoke ensemble of operations tailored to the specific input. This creates a highly expressive and efficient building block that can adapt its representational capacity and functional form at a granular, per-instance level.

ideas.title: Adaptive Task Weighting via Economic Portfolio Theory for Multi-Task Dense Prediction
ideas.description: This idea expands on integrating Focal Loss into a multi-task dense prediction head by introducing a dynamic, principled loss scaling mechanism drawn from Economic Portfolio Theory. In finance, Modern Portfolio Theory optimally allocates capital across assets by balancing their individual risks and covariances to manage overall portfolio risk. Analogously, in multi-task learning (for detection, segmentation, and depth), each task's loss is treated as an asset. The system continuously estimates each loss's uncertainty (akin to risk) and the covariance between task losses during training. These statistics are used to dynamically compute adaptive weights for scaling each task's loss contribution, forming a 'optimal learning portfolio' that minimizes combined training instability and maximizes joint convergence. This adapts the core financial solution to automatically balance hard and easy examples across tasks, going beyond static Focal Loss application.

ideas.title: Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Loss Function Reshaping in Imbalanced Domains
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework where a reinforcement learning agent is tasked with adaptively reshaping non-cross-entropy loss functions to handle severe class imbalance and complex error modes. Instead of merely selecting hard samples, the RL agent's action space consists of modulating the hyperparameters (e.g., margins, focusing parameters, scaling factors) of a chosen base loss function based on the current state of the model's performance across different data subgroups. This allows the training process to dynamically transition between, for instance, a margin-enforcing hinge loss for hard negatives and a softened, re-weighted variant for tail classes, all guided by a reward signal tied to holistic validation set improvement. The synergy lies in jointly learning both what to learn from (adaptive hard example mining) and how to learn from it (adaptive loss function geometry), creating a meta-optimization loop for the loss landscape itself.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Denoising Techniques for Dynamic Loss Weighting in Dense Prediction
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from geophysics, where adaptive filtering is used to amplify weak seismic signals against strong noise, this idea proposes to move beyond a static Focal Loss for dense prediction tasks like instance segmentation and dense pose estimation. The analogous solution involves dynamically tuning filter sensitivity based on local signal characteristics. Adapted to machine learning, this translates to a loss function that performs real-time, pixel-wise or region-wise weighting. The weighting would be conditioned on both the instantaneous prediction uncertainty (e.g., entropy) and the long-term class imbalance statistics, creating a dynamic feedback loop that more intelligently focuses the model's learning capacity on hard, rare, or critical spatial regions, potentially offering finer-grained control over class imbalance and task difficulty than a fixed focal parameter.

ideas.title: Model-Based Meta-Optimization with Smoothed Curriculum Rewards for Stable Hard Example Mining
ideas.description: This system addresses the instability of direct RL for sample mining by decoupling the mining policy from the noisy per-batch reward. A meta-controller, trained via meta-learning on historical training trajectories, predicts the long-term value of candidate samples. Its proposals are evaluated by an outer optimizer using a smoothed, curriculum-paced reward function that emphasizes consistent validation improvement over a rolling window. This hybrid approach stabilizes training, reduces variance, and ensures the mining strategy focuses on robust, generalizable detector progress rather than short-term metric exploitation.

ideas.title: Compositional Primitive Networks with Depth-Continuous Block Evolution
ideas.description: This design maintains a shared core block template for parameter efficiency and stable optimization, but allows layer-wise adaptation through the learned application of a small, fixed set of interpretable transformation primitives. A continuity regularizer on the primitive application parameters ensures blocks evolve smoothly across layers, preserving a coherent inductive bias and preventing chaotic architectural search. The system is trained end-to-end, allowing the network to discover depth-specific specializations—like early edge detection or late semantic aggregation—within a constrained, optimizable space.

ideas.title: Dynamic Gated Aggregation for Efficient ResNeXt Networks on Mobile Devices
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel family of efficient ResNeXt variants for mobile and embedded devices by fundamentally redesigning the core aggregation operation. Instead of the standard uniform summation of grouped convolution outputs, we explore dynamic, input-dependent gating mechanisms—such as lightweight attention, learned weighting, or sparse selection—to invert or reparameterize the aggregation. This approach allows the network to learn which transformation paths within a cardinal group are most relevant for a given input, reducing computational waste and memory access. The resulting architectures aim to achieve higher accuracy per FLOP and per parameter on resource-constrained hardware by making the feature combination process itself a source of efficiency.

ideas.title: Theoretically-Grounded RL for Adaptive Cardinality and Hard Example Mining
ideas.description: This research fuses adaptive hard example mining with theoretical cardinality analysis to create a novel, principled training paradigm. We develop a reinforcement learning agent whose action space includes not only which examples to select but also the optimal cardinality (batch size or set size) for each training stage, as dictated by a co-developed theoretical framework. The theoretical analysis provides formal constraints and reward-shaping guidance for the RL agent, explaining why certain cardinalities maximize learning signal from hard examples. This results in a dynamically optimized training curriculum that theoretically guarantees faster convergence and better final generalization than either approach alone.

ideas.title: Dynamic Focal Loss: Adaptive Tuning of Gamma and Alpha via Training-State Monitoring
ideas.description: This research develops a method to dynamically adapt the focusing parameter (gamma) and class balancing weight (alpha) of Focal Loss during training, rather than selecting static values. A lightweight meta-controller continuously analyzes the evolving distribution of prediction probabilities to assess the current concentration of hard and easy examples. Based on this real-time signal, it adjusts gamma to maintain an effective focus on misclassified samples and modulates alpha to counteract emerging class imbalance gradients. This approach ensures the loss function is continuously optimized for the model's instantaneous learning state, leading to more robust and efficient training convergence.

ideas.title: Adversarial Hard Mining via Immunological Affinity Maturation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from immunology and vaccine design, where scientists expose the immune system to adversarially engineered antigens to force a stronger, more generalized adaptive response—a process known as affinity maturation. The analogous solution is to not just generate adversarial hard negatives once, but to implement an iterative, closed-loop system where the current model state is used to 'evolve' synthetic data points that are progressively more challenging and specific to the model's current weaknesses. The adapted research idea is to create a training regimen where a generator network, guided by the learner's loss landscape, continuously produces these 'hardened' negatives, focusing the learner's capacity on these evolved hard cases. This mimics immunological adaptation, aiming to yield a model with more robust and generalizable feature representations through sustained exposure to adversarially mined data.

ideas.title: Hypernetwork-Guided Dynamic Topology for Adaptive Feature Transformation
ideas.description: This approach replaces fixed or arbitrarily varied topologies with a learned, context-aware routing mechanism. A small hypernetwork analyzes the input feature statistics and the specific transformation objective (e.g., downsampling, non-linearity) to generate the architectural parameters for that path's micro-architecture. This allows topology to vary intelligently across the network and across samples, adapting capacity where needed. The system is trained end-to-end, with the hypernetwork providing a strong regularizing prior that prevents chaotic architectural search and promotes reusable, semantically meaningful topology strategies.

ideas.title: Adaptive Neural Pathways via Evolvable Substrate
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo-Devo), where a core genetic toolkit is deployed contextually to produce diverse anatomical structures, this idea challenges the fixed-topology assumption in neural network blocks. The analogous solution involves organisms using shared regulatory genes (like Hox genes) to construct different organs (e.g., limbs, wings) based on positional and temporal signals during development. Adapted to deep learning, we propose an 'Adaptive Neural Pathways via Evolvable Substrate' method: each transformation path in a block dynamically selects its architecture from a shared, over-parameterized substrate of layer primitives. A lightweight gating network, conditioned on the input features and the transformation's stage, assembles the specific operations (e.g., choice of convolution, attention, or activation) for that path. This allows architecture to vary not just across paths but adaptively per input, breaking the fixed-topology constraint while reusing parameters efficiently across the network.

ideas.title: A Resource-Theoretic Framework for the Bounded Optimality of Feature and Representation Cardinality
ideas.description: This research develops a formal theoretical framework that models the relationship between cardinality (of features, model components, or representations) and downstream performance not as universally positive, but as subject to bounded optimality. The framework integrates factors like task complexity, data manifold structure, computational budget, and noise to derive conditions and phase transitions where increasing cardinality yields positive, negligible, or negative returns. The outcome is a predictive theory that specifies when and why to increase cardinality, providing principled guidance for architecture and representation design across machine learning domains.

ideas.title: Adapting Regularization Path Analysis to Challenge the Necessity of a Separate Class-Balancing Term
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that Focal Loss's class-balancing term (alpha) is a necessary, separate component by drawing an analogy from statistical regularization. In regression, regularization path analysis reveals whether a penalty term is essential by examining solutions across all its possible strengths. Adapted here, the research would involve training models with Focal Loss across a dense, two-dimensional grid of the alpha and gamma parameters, explicitly including the line where alpha=1 (the null hypothesis of no separate balancing). By analyzing the resulting performance landscape, loss contours, and model calibration across this continuum, we can empirically determine if optimal performance consistently requires deviating from alpha=1. This would test whether the focusing parameter (gamma) alone can provide adequate implicit class balancing, potentially simplifying the loss function and its hyperparameter tuning for certain problem domains.

ideas.title: Sequential Hybridization of Focal Loss with Curriculum Learning and Dynamic Sampling
ideas.description: This idea proposes integrating Focal Loss with sampling heuristics and a two-stage architecture, inspired by solutions in pedagogy for skill acquisition. The analogous field is Pedagogy and Educational Psychology, where a combined approach of structured curriculum (presenting concepts in increasing difficulty) followed by targeted practice on difficult areas is effective. The adapted research idea is a sequential hybridization: Stage 1 uses a curriculum learning schedule where training begins on a balanced subset, with Focal Loss applied as more challenging minority-class examples are gradually introduced. Stage 2 employs a dynamic sampling heuristic (e.g., hard example mining) directly informed by the per-example losses from Stage 1, focusing fine-tuning on the persistently misclassified minority instances. This creates a co-adaptive system where the loss function guides data selection, and selective data refines the loss landscape, aiming for further gains in handling severe class imbalance.

ideas.title: Joint Architecture-Training Search for Robust Cardinality and Network Co-Design
ideas.description: This research proposes a co-design framework that simultaneously searches for optimal cardinality and other architectural dimensions alongside critical training hyperparameters. It addresses the fatal flaw of treating cardinality in isolation by modeling the complex interactions within a unified optimization space. The system uses multi-fidelity evaluation and meta-learning to efficiently identify robust configurations that perform well across varied budgets and datasets. The outcome is a set of co-adapted network and training recipes where the value of cardinality is validated within a fully optimized context.

ideas.title: Stability-Governed Curriculum Learning with Consolidation Phases
ideas.description: This approach reframes the easy-example focus as a tool for stabilization, not primary learning. It employs a real-time, gradient-based metric to triage examples into categories that govern a dynamic training curriculum. The core learning signal remains driven by challenging, informative examples to ensure robust feature acquisition. However, the system triggers short, high-learning-rate consolidation phases when easy example stability is detected, rapidly integrating simple patterns while using gradient variance monitoring to exit before memorization begins, thereby improving convergence stability without sacrificing final model performance.

ideas.title: Adaptive Adversarial Curriculum Learning
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a closed-loop system that uses adversarial example generation not just to create static hard negatives, but to continuously probe the model's current failure boundary. The system then inverts its focus, scheduling training phases on confidently classified 'easy' examples to consolidate knowledge and stabilize parameters after aggressive hard mining. This adaptive oscillation between adversarial hardness and easy consolidation forms an automated curriculum, optimizing the pace of learning. The synergy enables a model to learn more efficiently from its own evolving weaknesses without catastrophic forgetting or instability.
